title: Carnival

infobox.value: (known as [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide") in certain localities) is a [festive](/wiki/Festive "Festive") [season](/wiki/Season "Season") that occurs at the close of the Christian [pre-Lenten period](/wiki/Pre-Lenten_period "Pre-Lenten period"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) consisting of [Quinquagesima](/wiki/Quinquagesima "Quinquagesima") or Shrove Sunday, [Shrove Monday](/wiki/Shrove_Monday "Shrove Monday"), and [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") or [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Christian festival before Lent

This article is about the Christian festival before Lent. For other uses, see [Carnival (disambiguation)](/wiki/Carnival_(disambiguation) "Carnival (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Consider [splitting](/wiki/Wikipedia:Splitting "Wikipedia:Splitting") content into sub-articles, [condensing](/wiki/Wikipedia:Summary_style "Wikipedia:Summary style") it, or adding [subheadings](/wiki/Help:Section#Subsections "Help:Section"). Please discuss this issue on the article's [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Carnival "Talk:Carnival"). *(December 2025)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Lingelbach_Karneval_in_Rom_001.jpg/330px-Lingelbach_Karneval_in_Rom_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lingelbach_Karneval_in_Rom_001.jpg)

Carnival in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), c. 1650

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Desfile_Portela_2014_%28906185%29.jpg/330px-Desfile_Portela_2014_%28906185%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Desfile_Portela_2014_(906185).jpg)

[Rio's Carnival](/wiki/Rio_Carnival "Rio Carnival") is the largest in the world according to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Carnival** (known as [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide") in certain localities) is a [festive](/wiki/Festive "Festive") [season](/wiki/Season "Season") that occurs at the close of the Christian [pre-Lenten period](/wiki/Pre-Lenten_period "Pre-Lenten period"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) consisting of [Quinquagesima](/wiki/Quinquagesima "Quinquagesima") or Shrove Sunday, [Shrove Monday](/wiki/Shrove_Monday "Shrove Monday"), and [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") or [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Carnival typically involves public [celebrations](/wiki/Party "Party"), including events such as [parades](/wiki/Parade "Parade"), public [street parties](/wiki/Street_party "Street party") and other entertainments, combining some elements of a [circus](/wiki/Circus "Circus"). Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity.[[4]](#cite_note-Bakhtin-4) Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol,[[5]](#cite_note-BarrowsRoom1991-5) meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste. This festival is known for being a time of great indulgence before Lent (which is a time stressing the opposite), with drinking, overeating, and various other activities of indulgence being performed. For example, [pancakes](/wiki/Pancake "Pancake"), donuts, and other desserts are prepared and eaten for a final time. During Lent, dairy and animal products are eaten less, if at all, and individuals make a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice"), thus giving up a certain object of desire (e.g. sweets), with the money that would go to purchase what was sacrificed being donated at the church as [alms](/wiki/Alms "Alms") for the poor.[[6]](#cite_note-Vanspall2021-6)

As such, during the season of Shrovetide, it is customary for Christians to ponder what Lenten sacrifices they will make for the coming Lent.[[7]](#cite_note-Kelvey2018-7) The traditions of carrying Shrovetide rods and consuming Shrovetide buns after attending church are celebrated.[[8]](#cite_note-ELCD2021-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Beadle1994-9) On the final day of the season, Shrove Tuesday, many traditional Christians, such as Lutherans, Anglicans, and Roman Catholics,[[10]](#cite_note-Walker2011-10) "make a special point of self-examination, of considering what wrongs they need to repent, and what amendments of life or areas of spiritual growth they especially need to ask God's help in dealing with."[[11]](#cite_note-11) During Shrovetide, many churches place a basket in the [narthex](/wiki/Narthex "Narthex") to collect the previous year's [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") palm branches that were blessed and distributed during the [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") liturgies. On Shrove Tuesday (the final day of Shrovetide), churches burn these [palms](/wiki/Palm_branch "Palm branch") to make the ashes used during the services held on the very next day, Ash Wednesday.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The term "Carnival" is traditionally used in areas with a large [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") presence, as well as in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"). The celebration is known as [Fastelavn](/wiki/Fastelavn "Fastelavn") in historically [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") countries.[[13]](#cite_note-Ruprecht2010-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) It is called Shrovetide in areas with a high concentration of [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans") ([Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")/[US Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)")), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists"), and other [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism").[[15]](#cite_note-Melitta_Weiss_Adamson,_Francine_Segan_2008-15) In Slavic [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") nations, [Maslenitsa](/wiki/Maslenitsa "Maslenitsa") is celebrated during the last week before [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"). In [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")-speaking [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), the Carnival season traditionally opens on 11/11 (often at 11:11 a.m.). This dates back to celebrations before the [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") season or with harvest celebrations of [St. Martin's Day](/wiki/St._Martin%27s_Day "St. Martin's Day").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/NavigiumIsidis.jpg/330px-NavigiumIsidis.jpg)](/wiki/File:NavigiumIsidis.jpg)

Feast of the *[Navigium Isidis](/wiki/Navigium_Isidis "Navigium Isidis")*, celebrated in [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") in honor of the goddess [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis")

The word *carnival* is said to come from the [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin") expression *carne levare*, which means "remove meat"; a [folk etymology](/wiki/Folk_etymology "Folk etymology") derives it from *carne vale*, "farewell to meat". In either case, this signifies the approaching fast of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").[[16]](#cite_note-Online_Etymology_Dictionary-16) The word *carne* may also be translated as flesh, producing "a farewell to the flesh", a phrase embraced by certain Carnival celebrants to embolden the festival's carefree spirit.[[16]](#cite_note-Online_Etymology_Dictionary-16)

Other scholars argue that the origin of the word is a common meat-based country feast (in Latin *carnualia*)[[17]](#cite_note-etimo-17) or the festival of the *[Navigium Isidis](/wiki/Navigium_Isidis "Navigium Isidis")* ("ship of Isis"), where the image of [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis") was carried to the seashore to bless the start of sailing season.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The festival consisted of a parade of masks following an adorned wooden boat, called in Latin *carrus navalis*,[[17]](#cite_note-etimo-17) possibly the source of both the name and the [parade floats](/wiki/Float_(parade) "Float (parade)").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Armed_owl_Louvre_CA2192.jpg/250px-Armed_owl_Louvre_CA2192.jpg)](/wiki/File:Armed_owl_Louvre_CA2192.jpg)

[Oinochoe](/wiki/Oinochoe "Oinochoe") depicting the parade of an armed owl during the celebration of the [Anthesteria](/wiki/Anthesteria "Anthesteria") ([Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), 410–390 BC)

The characteristics of the celebration of Carnival take their origins from ancient European festivals, such as the Greek [Dionysian](/wiki/Athenian_festivals "Athenian festivals") (the [Anthesteria](/wiki/Anthesteria "Anthesteria")) or the Roman [Saturnalia](/wiki/Saturnalia "Saturnalia"). During these festivities, there was a temporary release from social obligations and hierarchies to make way for the overthrow of order, joking and even debauchery. From a historical and religious point of view, the Carnival therefore represented a period of celebration, but above all of symbolic renewal, during which chaos replaced the established order, which, however, once the festive period was over, re-emerged new or renewed and guaranteed for a cycle valid until the beginning of the following Carnival.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

From an [anthropological](/wiki/Anthropological "Anthropological") point of view, Carnival is a reversal ritual, in which social roles are reversed and norms about desired behavior are suspended.[[20]](#cite_note-meertens.knaw.nl-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) During [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), winter was thought of as the reign of the winter spirits; these needed to be driven out in order for summer to return. Carnival can thus be regarded as a rite of passage from darkness to light, from winter to summer: a fertility celebration, the first spring festival of the new year.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Several [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") tribes celebrated the returning of the daylight. Winter would be driven out, to make sure that fertility could return in spring.[[20]](#cite_note-meertens.knaw.nl-20) A central figure of this ritual was possibly the fertility goddess [Nerthus](/wiki/Nerthus "Nerthus"). Also, there are some indications that the effigy of Nerthus[[23]](#cite_note-23) or [Freyr](/wiki/Freyr "Freyr") was placed on a ship with wheels and accompanied by a procession of people in animal disguise and men in women's clothes.[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Aboard the ship a marriage would be consummated as a [fertility ritual](/wiki/Fertility_ritual "Fertility ritual").[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus") wrote in his [*Germania*](/wiki/Germania_(book) "Germania (book)"): *Germania 9.6: Ceterum nec cohibere parietibus deos neque in ullam humani oris speciem adsimulare ex magnitudine caelestium arbitrator* – "The Germans, however, do not consider it consistent with the grandeur of celestial beings to confine the gods within walls, or to liken them to the form of any human countenance." *Germania 40: mox vehiculum et vestis et, si credere velis, numen ipsum secreto lacu abluitur* – "Afterwards the car, the vestments, and, if you like to believe it, the divinity herself, are purified in a secret lake."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Pieter_Bruegel_d._%C3%84._066.jpg/250px-Pieter_Bruegel_d._%C3%84._066.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pieter_Bruegel_d._%C3%84._066.jpg)

*[The Fight Between Carnival and Lent](/wiki/The_Fight_Between_Carnival_and_Lent "The Fight Between Carnival and Lent")*, [Pieter Bruegel the Elder](/wiki/Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder "Pieter Bruegel the Elder"), 1559

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), Carnival referred to a period following [Epiphany season](/wiki/Epiphanytide "Epiphanytide") that reached its climax before midnight on [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday").[[30]](#cite_note-Ruff2001-30) British historian [John Bossy](/wiki/John_Bossy "John Bossy"), in writing on the origin of the practices during Carnival, states that "These were, despite some appearances, Christian in character, and they were medieval in origin: although it has been widely supposed that they continued some kind of pre-Christian cult, there is in fact no evidence that they existed much before 1200."[[31]](#cite_note-Bossy1985-31) Because [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") was a period of [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting"), "Carnival therefore represented a last period of feasting and celebration before the spiritual rigors of Lent."[[30]](#cite_note-Ruff2001-30) Meat was plentiful during this part of the [Christian calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") and it was consumed during Carnival as people abstained from meat consumption during the following liturgical season, Lent.[[30]](#cite_note-Ruff2001-30) This strong opposition between Lent and Carnival was reflected in medieval literary culture, notably in the 13th century French poem *[La bataille de Caresme et de Charnage](/wiki/La_Bataille_de_Caresme_et_de_Charnage "La Bataille de Caresme et de Charnage") (The Fight Between Carnival and Lent),* which stages an allegorical conflict between festive excess and lenten austerity.

During Carnival, also known as Shrovetide, people confessed (shrived) their sins in preparation for Lent as well. Shakespeare's 1601 play, *[The Merchant of Venice](/wiki/The_Merchant_of_Venice "The Merchant of Venice")*, Scene 5 of Act 2, mentions Christians who painted their faces to celebrate the season:

> What, are there masques? Hear you me, Jessica:  
> Lock up my doors, and when you hear the drum  
> And the vile squealing of the wry-neck'd fife,  
> Clamber not you up to the casements then,  
> Nor thrust your head into the public street  
> To gaze on Christian fools with varnish'd faces.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Traditionally, a Carnival feast was the last opportunity for common people to eat well, as there was typically a food shortage at the end of the winter as stores ran out. Until spring produce was available, people were limited to the minimum necessary meals during this period. On what nowadays is called *vastenavond* (the days before [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting")), all the remaining winter stores of lard, butter, and meat which were left would be eaten, for these would otherwise soon start to rot and decay. The selected livestock had already been slaughtered in November and the meat would no longer be preservable. All the food that had survived the winter had to be eaten to assure that everyone was fed enough to survive until the coming spring would provide new food sources.[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)

Traditionally, the feast also was a time to indulge in [sexual desires](/wiki/Sexual_desire "Sexual desire"), which were supposed to be suppressed during the following period fasting.[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)[[33]](#cite_note-http-33) Before Lent began, all rich food and drink were consumed in what became a giant celebration that involved the whole community, and is thought to be the origin of Carnival.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In many Christian sermons and texts, the example of a vessel is used to explain Christian doctrine: "the nave of the church of baptism", "the ship of [Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus")", etc. The writings show that processions with ship-like carts were held and lavish feasts were celebrated on the eve of Lent or the greeting of spring in the early Middle Ages.[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27)

The Lenten period of the [liturgical calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar "Liturgical calendar"), the six weeks directly before [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), was historically marked by fasting, study, and other pious or penitential practices. During Lent, no parties or celebrations were held, and people refrained from eating rich foods, such as meat, dairy, fat, and sugar. The first three classes were often totally unavailable during this period because of late winter shortages.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

While Christian festivals such as [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)") were Church-sanctioned celebrations, Carnival was also a manifestation of European [folk culture](/wiki/Folk_culture "Folk culture"). In the Christian tradition, fasting is to commemorate the [40 days that Jesus fasted in the desert](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ"), according to the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), and also to reflect on Christian values. It was a time for [catechumens](/wiki/Catechumen "Catechumen") (those [converting to Christianity](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity")) to prepare for [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") at Easter.
[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Carnival in the Middle Ages took not just a few days, but almost the entire period between Christmas and the beginning of Lent. In those two months, Christian populations used their several holidays as an outlet for their daily frustrations.[[37]](#cite_note-Isgeschiedenis.nl-37)

Many synods and councils attempted to set things "right". [Caesarius of Arles](/wiki/Caesarius_of_Arles "Caesarius of Arles") (470–542) protested around 500 [CE](/wiki/Common_era "Common era") in his sermons against the pagan practices. Centuries later, his statements were adapted as the building blocks of the *[Indiculus superstitionum et paganiarum](/wiki/Indiculus_superstitionum_et_paganiarum "Indiculus superstitionum et paganiarum")* ("small index of superstitious and pagan practices"), which was drafted by the Synod of Leptines in 742. It condemned the *Spurcalibus en februario*.[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27)

Pope [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great") (590–604) decided that fasting would start on Ash Wednesday. The whole Carnival event was set before the fasting, to set a clear division between celebrations and penitence.[[38]](#cite_note-Halmo1989-38) He also dispatched missionaries to sanctify any excesses in popular Carnival customs.[[39]](#cite_note-Davidson2014-39) It was also the custom during Carnival that the ruling class would be playfully mocked using [masks](/wiki/Mask "Mask") and [disguises](/wiki/Disguise "Disguise").[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/2005_-_%27Gilles_de_Binche_portant_leur_Masque_de_Cire%27_-_Marie-Claire_Lef%C3%A9bure.jpg/250px-2005_-_%27Gilles_de_Binche_portant_leur_Masque_de_Cire%27_-_Marie-Claire_Lef%C3%A9bure.jpg)](/wiki/File:2005_-_%27Gilles_de_Binche_portant_leur_Masque_de_Cire%27_-_Marie-Claire_Lef%C3%A9bure.jpg)

Gilles of Binche (Belgium)

In the year 743, the [synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod") in Leptines (located near [Binche](/wiki/Binche "Binche") in Belgium) spoke out furiously against the excesses in the month of February.[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27) Also from the same period dates the phrase: "Whoever in February by a variety of less honorable acts tries to drive out winter is not a Christian, but a pagan." Confession books from around 800 contain more information about how people would dress as an animal or old woman during the festivities in January and February, even though this was a sin with no small penance.[[24]](#cite_note-fenvlaanderen.be-24)[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Also in Spain in the seventh century, San Isidoro de Sevilla complained in his writings about people coming out into the streets disguised, in many cases, as the opposite sex.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Denys_van_Alsloot_Skating_Masquerade%2C_or_Carnival_on_Ice_at_the_Kipdorppoort_Moats_in_Antwerp.jpg/250px-Denys_van_Alsloot_Skating_Masquerade%2C_or_Carnival_on_Ice_at_the_Kipdorppoort_Moats_in_Antwerp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Denys_van_Alsloot_Skating_Masquerade,_or_Carnival_on_Ice_at_the_Kipdorppoort_Moats_in_Antwerp.jpg)

Carnival on Ice at the Kipdorppoort Moats in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), c. 1620

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Giovanni_Domenico_Tiepolo_-_Carnival_Scene_%28The_Minuet%29_-_WGA22379.jpg/250px-Giovanni_Domenico_Tiepolo_-_Carnival_Scene_%28The_Minuet%29_-_WGA22379.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giovanni_Domenico_Tiepolo_-_Carnival_Scene_(The_Minuet)_-_WGA22379.jpg)

Carnival in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), by [Giovanni Domenico Tiepolo](/wiki/Giovanni_Domenico_Tiepolo "Giovanni Domenico Tiepolo"), 1750

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Carnavalsmaskers_Veneti%C3%AB.JPG/250px-Carnavalsmaskers_Veneti%C3%AB.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carnavalsmaskers_Veneti%C3%AB.JPG)

Typical [masks](/wiki/Mask "Mask") worn at the [Venice Carnival](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice"), which portray the [satirical](/wiki/Satire "Satire") and exaggerated appearances often used

Gradually, ecclesiastical authority began to realize that the desired result could not be achieved by banning Carnivals, which eventually led to a degree of [Christianization](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization") of the traditions. They then became part of the liturgy and the liturgical year.[[27]](#cite_note-ziggo1-27) In the Middle Ages, "Carnival and Lent were both necessary, inevitable episodes in the eternal cycle of the Church year."[[42]](#cite_note-Bowen2004-42) While forming an integral part of the Christian calendar, particularly in Catholic regions, many Carnival traditions resemble those antedating Christianity.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

While [medieval pageants](/wiki/Medieval_pageant "Medieval pageant") and festivals such as [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)") were church-sanctioned, Carnival was also a manifestation of medieval [folk culture](/wiki/Folk_culture "Folk culture"). Many local Carnival customs are claimed to derive from local pre-Christian rituals, such as elaborate rites involving masked figures in the [Swabian-Alemannic Fastnacht](/wiki/Swabian-Alemannic_Fastnacht "Swabian-Alemannic Fastnacht"). However, evidence is insufficient to establish a direct origin from Saturnalia or other ancient festivals. No complete accounts of Saturnalia survive, and the shared features of feasting, role reversals, temporary social equality, masks, and permitted rule-breaking do not necessarily constitute a coherent festival or link these festivals.[[44]](#cite_note-44) These similarities may represent a reservoir of cultural resources that can embody multiple meanings and functions. For example, Easter begins with the [resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"), followed by a liminal period, and ends with rebirth[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]. Carnival reverses this as King Carnival comes to life, and a liminal period follows before his death. Both feasts are calculated by the [lunar calendar](/wiki/Lunar_calendar "Lunar calendar"). Both Jesus and King Carnival may be seen as expiatory figures who make a gift to the people with their deaths. In the case of Jesus, the gift is [eternal life](/wiki/Eternal_life_(Christianity) "Eternal life (Christianity)") in [heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven"), and in the case of King Carnival, the acknowledgement that death is a necessary part of the cycle of life.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Besides [Christian anti-Judaism](/wiki/Anti-Judaism#Christian_anti-Judaism "Anti-Judaism"), the commonalities between church and Carnival rituals and imagery suggest a common root. In [early Christianity](/wiki/Anti-Judaism_in_early_Christianity "Anti-Judaism in early Christianity"), Christ is figured as the victim of [summary judgment](/wiki/Sanhedrin_trial_of_Jesus "Sanhedrin trial of Jesus"), and is tortured and executed by Romans before a [Jewish mob](/wiki/Jewish_deicide "Jewish deicide") ("His blood is on us and on our children!" [Matthew 27:24–25](https://www.biblica.com/bible/?osis=niv:Matthew%2027:24–25)).

[Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") processions in Spain include crowds who vociferously insult the figure of Jesus. Irreverence, parody, degradation, and laughter at a tragicomic [effigy](/wiki/Effigy "Effigy") of God can be seen as intensifications of the sacred order.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In 1466, in festivities sponsored by [Pope Paul II](/wiki/Pope_Paul_II "Pope Paul II"), Jews were forced to race naked through the streets of the city of Rome. Clement IX stopped these practices and in their place assessed a heavy tax on the Jews to help pay for the costs of the city's Carnival celebrations.[[47]](#cite_note-DIKertzer-47)

Some of the best-known traditions, including carnal [parades](/wiki/Parade "Parade") and [masquerade balls](/wiki/Masquerade_ball "Masquerade ball"), were first recorded in [medieval Italy](/wiki/Medieval_Italy "Medieval Italy"). The [Carnival of Venice](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice") was, for a long time, the most famous Carnival (although [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") abolished it in 1797 and only in 1979 was the tradition restored). From Italy, Carnival traditions spread to Spain, Portugal, and France, and from France to [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") in North America. From Spain and Portugal, it spread with colonization to the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). In the early 19th century in the German [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland") and [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands"), the weakened medieval tradition also revived. Continuously in the 18th and 19th centuries CE, as part of the annual abuse of the Carnival in Rome, [rabbis](/wiki/Rabbi "Rabbi") of the [ghetto](/wiki/Jewish_quarter_(diaspora) "Jewish quarter (diaspora)") were forced to march through the city streets wearing foolish guise, jeered upon and pelted by a variety of missiles from the crowd. A petition of the Jewish community of Rome sent in 1836 to [Pope Gregory XVI](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_XVI "Pope Gregory XVI") to stop the annual abuse was denied: "It is not opportune to make any innovation."[[47]](#cite_note-DIKertzer-47)

In the Rhineland in 1823, the first modern Carnival parade took place in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne").[[48]](#cite_note-Cologne1823-48) Carnaval (*Karneval*, *[Fasching](/wiki/Fasching "Fasching")* or *Fastnacht* in Germany) mixed pagan traditions with Christian traditions. Pre-Lenten celebrations featured parades, costumes and masks to endure Lent's withdrawal from worldly pleasures.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Th%C3%A9odore_G%C3%A9ricault_-_Riderless_Racers_at_Rome_-_Walters_37189.jpg/250px-Th%C3%A9odore_G%C3%A9ricault_-_Riderless_Racers_at_Rome_-_Walters_37189.jpg)](/wiki/File:Th%C3%A9odore_G%C3%A9ricault_-_Riderless_Racers_at_Rome_-_Walters_37189.jpg)

*Riderless Racers at Rome* by [Théodore Géricault](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_G%C3%A9ricault "Théodore Géricault"). From the mid-15th century until 1882, spring carnival in Rome closed with a [horse race](/wiki/Horse_race "Horse race"). Fifteen to 20 riderless horses, originally imported from the [Barbary Coast](/wiki/Barbary_Coast "Barbary Coast") of North Africa, ran the length of the [Via del Corso](/wiki/Via_del_Corso "Via del Corso"), a long, straight city street, in about 21⁄2 minutes.

Other areas developed their own traditions. In the United Kingdom, [West Indian](/wiki/West_Indian "West Indian") immigrants brought with them the traditions of [Caribbean Carnival](/wiki/Caribbean_Carnival "Caribbean Carnival"); however, the Carnivals now celebrated at [Notting Hill](/wiki/Notting_Hill_Carnival "Notting Hill Carnival"), [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds_West_Indian_Carnival "Leeds West Indian Carnival"), Yorkshire, and other places became divorced from their religious origin and became [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") events that take place in the summer months.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Theories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Theories")]

Interpretations of Carnival present it as a social institution that degrades or "uncrowns" the higher functions of thought, speech, and the soul by translating them into the [grotesque body](/wiki/Grotesque_body "Grotesque body"), which serves to renew society and the world,[[4]](#cite_note-Bakhtin-4) as a release for impulses that threaten the social order that ultimately reinforces social norms,[[50]](#cite_note-50) as a social transformation,[[51]](#cite_note-51) or as a tool for different groups to focus attention on conflicts and incongruities by embodying them in "senseless" acts.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Furthermore, some cultures use Carnival as a method of empowering themselves in spite of social conflicts. For example, when the [Caribbean Carnival](/wiki/List_of_Caribbean_carnivals_around_the_world "List of Caribbean carnivals around the world") was established as a result of French settlers, even the slaves had their version of the [masquerade](/wiki/Masquerade_ball "Masquerade ball"), where they would reverse roles to mock those of higher social status.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Along with empowering individuals for a period of time, despite their typical status, Carnival brings communities together. In a day where all are meant to perform a "mask" that differs from their typical identity, all members of a society are able to connect through their theatricality and satire.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[Mircea Eliade](/wiki/Mircea_Eliade "Mircea Eliade"), historian of religions, writes: "Any new year is a revival of time at its beginning, a repetition of the cosmogony. Ritual fights between two groups of extras, the presence of the dead, [Saturnalia](/wiki/Saturnalia "Saturnalia") and orgies, are all elements which indicate that at the end of the year and in the expectation of the new year the mythical moments of the passage of chaos to the cosmogony are repeated".[[55]](#cite_note-55) Eliade also writes: "Then the dead will come back, because all barriers between the dead and the living are broken (is the primordial chaos not revived?), and will come back since – at this paradoxical moment – time will be interrupted, so that the dead may be again contemporaries of the living." Eliade speculates that people have "a deep need to regenerate themselves periodically by abolishing the elapsed time and making topical the cosmogony". As regards masks (monsters, animals, demons), he assigns them an [apotropaic](/wiki/Apotropaic "Apotropaic") meaning.

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Africa")]

For the Canary Islands, see [its section](#Canary_Islands) under Europe.

#### Cape Verde Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cape Verde Islands")]

Carnival was introduced by Portuguese settlers. It is celebrated on each of the archipelago's nine inhabited islands. In [Mindelo](/wiki/Mindelo "Mindelo"), [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_Cape_Verde "São Vicente, Cape Verde"), groups challenge each other for a yearly prize. It has imported various Brazilian Carnival traditions. The celebration in [São Nicolau](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Nicolau,_Cape_Verde "São Nicolau, Cape Verde") is more traditional, where established groups parade through the [Ribeira Brava](/wiki/Ribeira_Brava,_Cape_Verde "Ribeira Brava, Cape Verde"), gathering in the [town square](/wiki/Town_square "Town square"), although it has adopted drums, floats and costumes from Brazil. In São Nicolau, three groups, Copa Cabana, Estrela Azul, and Brilho Da Zona, construct a painted float using fire, newspaper for the mold, and iron and steel for structure. Carnival São Nicolau is celebrated over three days: dawn Saturday, Sunday afternoon, and Tuesday.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The celebrations are captured in the award-winning feature documentary [Tchindas](/wiki/Tchindas "Tchindas"), nominated at the [12th Africa Movie Academy Awards](/wiki/12th_Africa_Movie_Academy_Awards "12th Africa Movie Academy Awards").

#### Namibia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Namibia")]

Carnival was introduced by German settlers. The celebration is based on the "Rheinische" Carnival tradition.

#### Seychelles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Seychelles")]

The [Seychelles](/wiki/Seychelles "Seychelles") carnival began in 2011. It is held in the capital city of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Seychelles "Victoria, Seychelles") and takes place over three days. On Day 1, the grand opening is held in the city center near the clock tower. The second day is parade day. On Day 3, the closing ceremony is held, and a lottery winner is announced.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

#### Zimbabwe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Zimbabwe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Harare_Carnival_Dancer.jpg/250px-Harare_Carnival_Dancer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harare_Carnival_Dancer.jpg)

Harare Carnival dancer

The [Harare](/wiki/Harare "Harare") Carnival is held late in May. Events include fashion and music shows. The climax is a street party featuring costumes and music.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Americas")]

#### Antigua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Antigua")]

Main article: [Antigua Carnival](/wiki/Antigua_Carnival "Antigua Carnival")

The [Antiguan](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua") Carnival is held from the end of July to the first Tuesday in August. The most important day is that of the *j'ouvert* (or *juvé*), in which brass and [steelpan](/wiki/Steelpan "Steelpan") bands perform. [Barbuda](/wiki/Barbuda "Barbuda")'s Carnival, held in June, is known as "Caribana". The Antiguan and Barbudan Carnivals replaced the Old Time Christmas Festival in 1957, with hopes of inspiring tourism.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

#### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Argentina")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Carnaval_en_colegiales_ni%C3%B1os.jpg/250px-Carnaval_en_colegiales_ni%C3%B1os.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnaval_en_colegiales_ni%C3%B1os.jpg)

Carnival in Argentina by school children

In [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), the most representative Carnival performed is the so-called [Murga](/wiki/Murga "Murga"), although other famous Carnivals, more like Brazil's, are held in Argentine [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia,_Argentina "Mesopotamia, Argentina") and the North-East. [Gualeguaychú](/wiki/Gualeguaych%C3%BA,_Entre_R%C3%ADos "Gualeguaychú, Entre Ríos") in the east of [Entre Ríos Province](/wiki/Entre_R%C3%ADos_Province "Entre Ríos Province") is the most important Carnival city and has one of the largest parades. It adopts a musical background similar to Brazilian or Uruguayan Carnival. [Corrientes](/wiki/Corrientes "Corrientes") is another city with a Carnival tradition. [Chamamé](/wiki/Chamam%C3%A9 "Chamamé") is a popular musical style. In all major cities and many towns throughout the country, Carnival is celebrated.[[61]](#cite_note-Mohsin-61)

The Humahuaca Carnival is a popular celebration, which takes place at the beginning of Lent (40 days before Holy Week), in the Quebrada de Humahuaca area, Jujuy province, Argentina.

As Carnival coincides with summer in the Southern Hemisphere, in many parts of Argentina children play with water. The 19th century tradition of filling empty egg shells with water has evolved into water games that include the throwing of [water balloons](/wiki/Water_balloon "Water balloon").[[61]](#cite_note-Mohsin-61)

#### Aruba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Aruba")]

Carnival in [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba") means weeks of events that bring colourfully decorated floats, contagiously throbbing music, luxuriously costumed groups of celebrants of all ages, King and Queen elections, electrifying jump-ups and torchlight parades, the Jouvert morning: the Children's Parades, and finally the Grand Parade. Aruba's biggest celebration is a month-long affair consisting of festive "jump-ups" (street parades), spectacular parades, and creative contests. Music and flamboyant costumes play a central role, from the Queen elections to the Grand Parade. Street parades continue in various districts throughout the month, with brass band, [steelpan](/wiki/Steelpan "Steelpan") and [roadmarch](/wiki/Roadmarch "Roadmarch") tunes. On the evening before Lent, Carnival ends with the symbolic burning of King Momo.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### Bahamas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Bahamas")]

[Junkanoo](/wiki/Junkanoo "Junkanoo") is the principal street parade in the Bahamas, it has been practiced in the Bahamas before and after the 1834 emancipation of slavery in the British Empire.

The [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas") announced the first Bahamas Junkanoo Carnival to commence in May 2015.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Carnival in the Bahamas rivals various carnivals throughout the Caribbean in that it is a unique blend between the revered Junkanoo and traditional Carnival. This fairly new festival has been referred to as the ultimate celebration of everything Bahamian.

#### Barbados

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Barbados")]

Main article: [Crop over](/wiki/Crop_over "Crop over")

"Crop Over" (formerly called "Harvest Home") is a traditional harvest festival celebrated in [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"). Its early beginnings were on the [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane") [plantations](/wiki/Plantation "Plantation") during the colonial period. Crop Over began in 1688, and featured singing, dancing, and accompaniment by [shak-shak](/wiki/Shak-shak "Shak-shak"), [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), [triangle](/wiki/Triangle_(musical_instrument) "Triangle (musical instrument)"), [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), guitar, bottles filled with water, and bones. Other traditions included climbing a greased pole, feasting, and drinking competitions. Originally signaling the end of the yearly cane harvest, it evolved into a national festival. In the late 20th century, Crop Over began to closely mirror the Trinidad Carnival. Beginning in June, Crop Over runs until the first Monday in August when it culminates in the finale, the Grand Kadooment.[[64]](#cite_note-:4-64)

Crop Over time for many islanders is one big party. Craft markets, food tents/stalls, street parties, and cavalcades fill every week.[[64]](#cite_note-:4-64)

A major feature is the [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music") competition. Calypso music, originating in Trinidad, uses syncopated rhythm and topical lyrics. It offers a medium in which to satirise local politics, amidst the general bacchanal. Calypso tents, also originating in Trinidad, feature cadres of musicians who perform biting social commentaries, political exposés or rousing exhortations to "wuk dah waistline" and "roll dat bumper". The groups compete for the Calypso Monarch Award, while the air is redolent with the smells of [Bajan](/wiki/Barbadian_cuisine "Barbadian cuisine") cooking during the [Bridgetown](/wiki/Bridgetown "Bridgetown") Market Street Fair. The Cohobblopot Festival blends dance, drama, and music with the crowning of the King and Queen of costume bands. Every evening the "Pic-o-de-Crop" Show is performed after the King of Calypso is finally crowned. The climax of the festival is Kadooment Day, celebrated with a national holiday, when costume bands fill the streets with pulsating Barbadian rhythms and fireworks.[[64]](#cite_note-:4-64)

#### Belize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Belize")]

Main article: [Carnival in Belize](/wiki/Carnival_in_Belize "Carnival in Belize")

[San Pedro](/wiki/San_Pedro_Town "San Pedro Town") is one of [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize")'s few cities to observe Carnaval before Lent. Elsewhere, Carnaval (sometimes referred to as Carnival) often occurs in September. The Fiesta de Carnaval is often the most popular celebration, usually held over three days prior to Ash Wednesday, but the festivities often extend to the full week.[[65]](#cite_note-65) This festival "always includes music, dancing, costumes and parades".[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[Comparsas](/wiki/Comparsa "Comparsa") are held throughout the week, consisting of large groups "of dancers dancing and traveling on the streets, followed by a Carrosa (carriage) where the musicians play. The Comparsa is a development of African processions where groups of devotees follow a given saint or deity during a particular religious celebration." One of the most popular comparsas of Fiesta de Carnaval is the male group comparsa, usually composed of notable men from the community who dress up in outlandish costumes or cross-dress and dance to compete for money and prizes.[[67]](#cite_note-goambergriscaye.com-67) Other popular activities include body painting and flour fighting.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-joseluiszapata.com-69) "On the last day of Carnival painters flood the street to paint each other. This simply means that a mixture of water paint and water or raw eggs is used to paint people on the streets, the goal being to paint as many people as you can."[[67]](#cite_note-goambergriscaye.com-67)

Street fights often occur during the festivities – some locals treat this festival as an opportunity to exact revenge on their enemies. [Vandalism](/wiki/Vandalism "Vandalism") is common and "businesses constantly have to prepare in covering or repainting their advertisements during Carnival season because of the mischief performed."[[70]](#cite_note-70) The tradition continues despite critics who advocate the termination of these festivities.[[69]](#cite_note-joseluiszapata.com-69)

#### Bolivia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Bolivia")]

Main article: [Carnaval de Oruro](/wiki/Carnaval_de_Oruro "Carnaval de Oruro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Diablada_oruro_fraternidad.jpg/250px-Diablada_oruro_fraternidad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diablada_oruro_fraternidad.jpg)

The [Diablada](/wiki/Oruro_Diablada "Oruro Diablada"), dance primeval, the typical and main dance of [Carnaval de Oruro](/wiki/Carnaval_de_Oruro "Carnaval de Oruro"), a [Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Masterpieces_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity") since 2001 in Bolivia (Image: *Fraternidad Artística y Cultural "La Diablada"*)

*La Diablada* Carnival takes place in [Oruro](/wiki/Oruro,_Bolivia "Oruro, Bolivia") in central [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"). It is celebrated in honor of the miners' patron saint, *Vírgen de Socavon* (the Virgin of the Tunnels).[[71]](#cite_note-71) Over 50 parade groups dance, sing, and play music over a five kilometre-long course. Participants dress up as demons, devils, angels, Incas, and Spanish [conquistadors](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador"). Dances include [caporales](/wiki/Caporales "Caporales") and [tinkus](/wiki/Tinku "Tinku"). The parade runs from morning until late at night, 18 hours a day, for three days before Ash Wednesday. It was declared the 2001 "Masterpieces of Oral Heritage and Intangible Heritage of Humanity" by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[72]](#cite_note-72) Throughout the country, celebrations are held involving traditional rhythms and water parties. In [Santa Cruz de la Sierra](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_la_Sierra "Santa Cruz de la Sierra"), on the east side of the country, tropical weather allows a Brazilian-type Carnival, with [Comparsas](/wiki/Comparsa "Comparsa") dancing traditional songs in matching uniforms.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Brazil")]

Main article: [Brazilian Carnival](/wiki/Brazilian_Carnival "Brazilian Carnival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Galo_da_Madrugada%2C_Carnival_2014_-_Recife%2C_Pernambuco%2C_Brazil.jpg/250px-Galo_da_Madrugada%2C_Carnival_2014_-_Recife%2C_Pernambuco%2C_Brazil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Galo_da_Madrugada,_Carnival_2014_-_Recife,_Pernambuco,_Brazil.jpg)

Recife Carnival, in [Recife](/wiki/Recife "Recife"), [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco"), Brazil

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Bloco_da_camisinha_circuito_Campo_Grande_Salvador.jpg/250px-Bloco_da_camisinha_circuito_Campo_Grande_Salvador.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bloco_da_camisinha_circuito_Campo_Grande_Salvador.jpg)

Carnival circuit of [Salvador, Bahia](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia"), Brazil

The Carnival in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") (Portuguese: Carnaval) is a major part of Brazilian culture. The first expression of this festivity took place in Rio de Janeiro in 1641, with the *préstitos*, very similar to musical processions that were held on public streets when [John IV of Portugal](/wiki/John_IV_of_Portugal "John IV of Portugal") was crowned King.

##### Rio de Janeiro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Rio de Janeiro")]

Main article: [Rio Carnival](/wiki/Rio_Carnival "Rio Carnival")

The street carnival of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") is designated by *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as the largest carnival in the world, with approximately two million people each day.[[74]](#cite_note-guin-74)

[Samba schools](/wiki/Samba_school "Samba school") are large, social entities with thousands of members and a theme for their song and parade each year. In [Rio Carnival](/wiki/Rio_Carnival "Rio Carnival"), samba schools parade in the [Sambadrome](/wiki/Sambadrome "Sambadrome") (*sambódromo* in Portuguese). Some of the most famous include [GRES Estação Primeira de Mangueira](/wiki/GRES_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Primeira_de_Mangueira "GRES Estação Primeira de Mangueira"), [GRES Portela](/wiki/GRES_Portela "GRES Portela"), [GRES Acadêmicos do Salgueiro](/wiki/GRES_Acad%C3%AAmicos_do_Salgueiro "GRES Acadêmicos do Salgueiro"), [GRES Imperatriz Leopoldinense](/wiki/GRES_Imperatriz_Leopoldinense "GRES Imperatriz Leopoldinense"), [GRES Beija-Flor de Nilópolis](/wiki/GRES_Beija-Flor_de_Nil%C3%B3polis "GRES Beija-Flor de Nilópolis"), [GRES Mocidade Independente de Padre Miguel](/wiki/GRES_Mocidade_Independente_de_Padre_Miguel "GRES Mocidade Independente de Padre Miguel"), and recently, [Unidos da Tijuca](/wiki/Unidos_da_Tijuca "Unidos da Tijuca") and [GRES União da Ilha do Governador](/wiki/GRES_Uni%C3%A3o_da_Ilha_do_Governador "GRES União da Ilha do Governador"). Local tourists pay $500–950, depending on the costume, to buy a samba costume and dance in the parade. *Blocos* are small informal groups with a definite theme in their samba, usually satirizing the political situation. About 30 schools in Rio gather hundreds of thousands of participants. More than 440 *blocos* operate in Rio. *Bandas* are samba musical bands, also called "street carnival bands", usually formed within a single neighborhood or musical background. The Carnival industry chain amassed in 2012 almost US$1 billion in revenues.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

##### Recife, Pernambuco

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Recife, Pernambuco")]

[Recife](/wiki/Recife "Recife") is marked by the parade of the largest carnival block in the world, the [Galo da Madrugada](/wiki/Galo_da_Madrugada "Galo da Madrugada"). This parade happens on the first Saturday of Carnival (Zé Pereira's Saturday), passes through downtown Recife, and has as symbol a giant rooster that is positioned on the Duarte Coelho Bridge. In this block, there is a great variety of musical genres, but [Frevo](/wiki/Frevo "Frevo") is the most common one; it is typical of both Recife and [Olinda](/wiki/Olinda "Olinda"), and is considered an Intangible Heritage of Humanity by Unesco.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### Salvador, Bahia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Salvador, Bahia")]

Main article: [Bahian Carnival](/wiki/Bahian_Carnival "Bahian Carnival")

[Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia") has large Carnival celebrations, including the [Axé](/wiki/Ax%C3%A9 "Axé"), a typical Bahia music. A truck with giant speakers and a platform, where musicians play songs of local genres such as Axé, [samba-reggae](/wiki/Samba-reggae "Samba-reggae"), and [Arrocha](/wiki/Arrocha "Arrocha"), drives through town with a crowd following while dancing and singing. It was originally staged by two Salvador musicians, Dodo & Osmar, in the 1950s. After the [Salvador Carnival](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia#Carnival "Salvador, Bahia"), [Porto Seguro](/wiki/Porto_Seguro "Porto Seguro") continues the celebration.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Three circuits make up the festival. Campo Grande is the longest and most traditional. Barra-Ondina is the most famous, on the seaside of Pelourinho and the beaches Barra and Ondina.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

International singers like [David Guetta](/wiki/David_Guetta "David Guetta"), [will.i.am](/wiki/Will.i.am "Will.i.am"), [Psy](/wiki/Psy "Psy"), and [Bob Sinclar](/wiki/Bob_Sinclar "Bob Sinclar") have performed in Salvador.[[78]](#cite_note-78) [Ivete Sangalo](/wiki/Ivete_Sangalo "Ivete Sangalo"), [Claudia Leitte](/wiki/Claudia_Leitte "Claudia Leitte"), [Daniela Mercury](/wiki/Daniela_Mercury "Daniela Mercury"), [Margareth Menezes](/wiki/Margareth_Menezes "Margareth Menezes"), [Chiclete com Banana](/wiki/Chiclete_com_Banana "Chiclete com Banana"), and [Banda Eva](/wiki/Banda_Eva "Banda Eva") are some traditional attractions. The party officially lasts six days, but may go on for longer.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Canada")]

[Toronto Caribbean Carnival](/wiki/Toronto_Caribbean_Carnival "Toronto Caribbean Carnival"),[[80]](#cite_note-80) held in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") on the first weekend of August to take advantage of more comfortable weather, has its origins in Caribbean Carnival traditions. Tourist attendance at the parade typically exceeds one million.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The [Quebec Winter Carnival](/wiki/Quebec_Winter_Carnival "Quebec Winter Carnival") is one of the biggest winter-themed Carnivals in the world.[[82]](#cite_note-82) It depends on snowfall and very cold weather, to keep snowy [ski](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing") trails in good condition and [ice sculptures](/wiki/Ice_sculpture "Ice sculpture") frozen. The carnival is held during the last days of January and first days of February.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

* [![Caribbean Carnival in Toronto, Ontario, Canada](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Posing_at_2018_Toronto_Caribbean_Carnival_%2828969518847%29.jpg/250px-Posing_at_2018_Toronto_Caribbean_Carnival_%2828969518847%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Posing_at_2018_Toronto_Caribbean_Carnival_(28969518847).jpg "Caribbean Carnival in Toronto, Ontario, Canada")

  Caribbean Carnival in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), Canada

#### Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Caribbean")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Carnaval_Bonaire_%281%29.jpg/250px-Carnaval_Bonaire_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnaval_Bonaire_(1).jpg)

Carnival in [Rincon, Bonaire](/wiki/Rincon,_Bonaire "Rincon, Bonaire")

Main article: [Carnival in the Caribbean](/wiki/Carnival_in_the_Caribbean "Carnival in the Caribbean")

Most [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") islands celebrate Carnival. The largest and most well-known is in [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"). [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua"), [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba"), [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), [Bonaire](/wiki/Bonaire "Bonaire"), [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands "Cayman Islands"), [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao "Curaçao"), [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica"), [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada"), [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), [Saba](/wiki/Saba_(island) "Saba (island)"), [Sint Eustatius](/wiki/Sint_Eustatius "Sint Eustatius") (Statia), [Sint Maarten](/wiki/Sint_Maarten "Sint Maarten"), [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia"), [Saint Kitts](/wiki/Saint_Kitts "Saint Kitts"), [Saint Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/Saint_Thomas,_U.S._Virgin_Islands "Saint Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands"), [Saint Vincent](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_(Antilles) "Saint Vincent (Antilles)"), and the [Grenadines](/wiki/Grenadines "Grenadines") hold lengthy carnival seasons and large celebrations.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Carnival is an important cultural event in the [Dutch Caribbean](/wiki/Dutch_Caribbean "Dutch Caribbean"). Festivities include "jump-up" parades with beautifully colored costumes, floats, and live bands, as well as [beauty contests](/wiki/Beauty_contests "Beauty contests") and other competitions. Celebrations include a middle-of-the-night [j'ouvert](/wiki/J%27ouvert "J'ouvert") (*juvé*) parade that ends at sunrise with the burning of a straw [King Momo](/wiki/King_Momo "King Momo"), cleansing sins and bad luck. On Statia, he is called Prince Stupid.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Carnival has been celebrated in Cuba since the 18th century. Participants don costumes demonstrating the island's cultural and ethnic variety.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Colombia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Colombia")]

Main article: [Carnival in Colombia](/wiki/Carnival_in_Colombia "Carnival in Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Congos_carnaval_de_Barranquilla2024-02-14.jpg/250px-Congos_carnaval_de_Barranquilla2024-02-14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Congos_carnaval_de_Barranquilla2024-02-14.jpg)

Congos troupe in [Barranquilla carnival](/wiki/Barranquilla_Carnival "Barranquilla Carnival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/DIOSESANCESTRALES_HUGOMONCAYO2007_4.jpg/250px-DIOSESANCESTRALES_HUGOMONCAYO2007_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:DIOSESANCESTRALES_HUGOMONCAYO2007_4.jpg)

The [Blacks and Whites' Carnival](/wiki/Blacks_and_Whites%27_Carnival "Blacks and Whites' Carnival") in [Pasto, Colombia](/wiki/Pasto,_Colombia "Pasto, Colombia")

Carnival was introduced by the Spaniards and incorporated elements from [European cultures](/wiki/European_culture "European culture"). It has managed to reinterpret traditions that belonged to Colombia's African and [Amerindian](/wiki/Amerindian "Amerindian") cultures. Documentary evidence shows that Carnival existed in Colombia in the 18th century and had already been a cause for concern for colonial authorities, who censored the celebrations, especially in the main political centres such as [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia"), [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá"), and [Popayán](/wiki/Popay%C3%A1n "Popayán").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Carnival continued its evolution in small/unimportant towns out of view of the rulers. The result was the uninterrupted celebration of Carnival festivals in [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla") (see [Barranquilla's Carnival](/wiki/Barranquilla%27s_Carnival "Barranquilla's Carnival")), now recognized as one of the Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity. The Barranquilla Carnival includes several parades on Friday and Saturday nights beginning on 11 January and ending with a six-day non-stop festival, beginning the Wednesday prior to Ash Wednesday and ending Tuesday midnight. Other celebrations occur in villages along the lower [Magdalena River](/wiki/Magdalena_River "Magdalena River") in northern Colombia, and in [Pasto](/wiki/Pasto,_Colombia "Pasto, Colombia") and [Nariño](/wiki/Nari%C3%B1o "Nariño") (see [Blacks and Whites' Carnival](/wiki/Blacks_and_Whites%27_Carnival "Blacks and Whites' Carnival")) in the south of the country. In the early 20th century, attempts to introduce Carnival in Bogotá were rejected by the government. The [Bogotá Carnival](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1_Carnival "Bogotá Carnival") was renewed in the 21st century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Dominica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Dominica")]

Main article: [Chanté mas](/wiki/Chant%C3%A9_mas "Chanté mas")

Carnival in [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica") is held in the capital city of [Roseau](/wiki/Roseau "Roseau"),[[86]](#cite_note-Dreisinger_2015_h711-86) and takes elements of Carnival that can be seen in the neighboring French islands of [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique") and [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe"), as well as [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidad "Trinidad"). Notable events leading up to Carnival include the Opening of Carnival celebrations, the Calypso Monarch music competition, the Queen of Carnival Beauty Pageant,[[87]](#cite_note-87) and [bouyon music](/wiki/Bouyon_music "Bouyon music") bands.[[88]](#cite_note-St_Vincent_Times_2023_u249-88) Celebrations last for the Monday and Tuesday before Ash Wednesday.[[89]](#cite_note-Scott_2023_b521-89)

#### Dominican Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Dominican Republic")]

Main article: [Carnival in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Carnival_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Carnival in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Cojuelo03.JPG/250px-Cojuelo03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cojuelo03.JPG)

Traditional cojuelo mask of the Dominican carnival in [La Vega](/wiki/La_Vega,_Dominican_Republic "La Vega, Dominican Republic"), [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic")

[Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic") Carnival is celebrated in most cities and towns in the main streets during February. Among its main characteristics are its flashy costumes and loud music. The one held in [La Vega](/wiki/La_Vega,_Dominican_Republic "La Vega, Dominican Republic"), which is one of the biggest in the country, and the national parade in [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo") were where the first Carnival of the Americas was held.[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)

Carnival masks are elaborate and colorful. The costumes used on the parades are satires of the Devil and are called "Diablos Cojuelos". They dance, and run to the rhythm of [merengue music](/wiki/Merengue_music "Merengue music") mixed with techno, hip-hop, and [reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton"). Additional [allegorical](/wiki/Allegorical "Allegorical") characters represent Dominican traditions such as "Roba la Gallina" and "Califé".[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)

One of the most international parades is in [San Pedro de Macorís](/wiki/San_Pedro_de_Macor%C3%ADs "San Pedro de Macorís"). It exhibits the "Guloyas" parade of costumed groups dancing in the streets. Revelers flee from the "Diablos Cojuelos" who try to hit them with "Vejigas".[[90]](#cite_note-:5-90)

The timing of the festivals has grown apart from its original religious synchronization with the period of Lent. With National Independence Day on 27 February and the birthday of [Juan Pablo Duarte](/wiki/Juan_Pablo_Duarte "Juan Pablo Duarte"), its founding father, on 26 January, the Carnival celebrations fill February regardless of the [Lenten calendar](/wiki/Lenten_calendar "Lenten calendar").[[91]](#cite_note-91)

#### Ecuador

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ecuador")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Desfile_de_Carnaval_en_calles_de_Latacunga.jpg/250px-Desfile_de_Carnaval_en_calles_de_Latacunga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Desfile_de_Carnaval_en_calles_de_Latacunga.jpg)

Carnival parade in [Latacunga](/wiki/Latacunga "Latacunga"), Ecuador

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Comparsa_latacungue%C3%B1a_desfilando.jpg/250px-Comparsa_latacungue%C3%B1a_desfilando.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comparsa_latacungue%C3%B1a_desfilando.jpg)

Parade in Latacunga, Ecuador

In [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), the celebrations began before the arrival of Catholicism. The Huarangas Indians (from the Chimbos nation) used to celebrate the second moon of the year with a festival at which they threw flour, flowers, and perfumed water. This Indigenous tradition merged with the Catholic celebration of Carnival.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

A common feature of Ecuadorian Carnival is the *diablitos* (little devils) who play with water. As with snowball fights, the practice of throwing or dumping water on unsuspecting victims is revered by children and teenagers although feared by some adults. Throwing water balloons, sometimes even eggs and flour both to friends and strangers is fun, but can also upset the uninformed.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Although the government as well as school authorities forbid such games, they are widely practiced. Historians tell of a bishop in 1867 who threatened [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") for the [sin](/wiki/Sin "Sin") of playing Carnival games.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Festivals differ across the country. Locals wear disguises with colorful masks and dance. Usually, the celebrations begin with the election of *Taita Carnival* (Father Carnival) who heads the festivities and leads the parades in each city.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

The most famed Carnival festivities are in [Guaranda](/wiki/Guaranda "Guaranda") (Bolivar province) and [Ambato](/wiki/Ambato,_Ecuador "Ambato, Ecuador") (Tungurahua province). In Ambato, the festivities are called *Fiesta de las Flores y las Frutas* (Festival of the Flowers and Fruits). Other cities have revived Carnival traditions with colorful parades, such as in [Azogues](/wiki/Azogues "Azogues") (Cañar Province). In Azogues and the Southern [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") in general, *Taita Carnival* is always an Indigenous [Cañari](/wiki/Ca%C3%B1ari "Cañari"). Recently, a celebration has gained prominence in the northern part of the Andes in the [Chota Valley](/wiki/Chota_Valley "Chota Valley") in [Imbabura](/wiki/Imbabura_Province "Imbabura Province") which is a zone of a strong [Afro-Ecuadorian](/wiki/Afro-Ecuadorian "Afro-Ecuadorian") population and so the Carnival is celebrated with [bomba del chota](/wiki/Bomba_(Ecuador) "Bomba (Ecuador)") music.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

[Latacunga](/wiki/Latacunga "Latacunga") celebrates Carnival in three manners:[[96]](#cite_note-96) Carnival with water where people play with water, religious Carnival where people make religious festivity, and Carnival parade in the city in which people march on the Latacunga streets wearing masks while they dance with music bands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### French Guiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: French Guiana")]

Main article: [Carnival in French Guiana](/wiki/Carnival_in_French_Guiana "Carnival in French Guiana")

The Carnival of [French Guiana](/wiki/French_Guiana "French Guiana") has roots in [Creole](/wiki/Creole_peoples "Creole peoples") culture. Everyone participates – mainland French, Brazilians (Guiana has a frontier with Brazil), and Chinese as well as Creoles.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

Its duration is variable, determined by movable religious festivals: Carnival begins at [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") and ends on Ash Wednesday, and so typically lasts through most of January and February. During this period, from Friday evening until Monday morning the entire country throbs to the rhythm of masked balls and street parades.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

Friday afternoons are for eating *galette des rois* (the cake of kings) and drinking champagne. The cake may be flavoured with [frangipani](/wiki/Frangipani "Frangipani"), [guava](/wiki/Guava "Guava"), or [coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On Sunday afternoons, major parades fill the streets of [Cayenne](/wiki/Cayenne "Cayenne"), [Kourou](/wiki/Kourou "Kourou"), and Saint-Laurent du Maroni. Competing groups prepare for months. Dressed to follow the year's agreed theme, they march with Carnival floats, drums, and brass bands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Brazilian groups are appreciated for their elaborate feathered and sequined costumes. However, they are not eligible for competition since the costumes do not change over time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Mythical characters appear regularly in the parades:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* *Karolin* − a small person dressed in a [magpie](/wiki/Magpie "Magpie") tail and [top hat](/wiki/Top_hat "Top hat"), riding on a [shrew](/wiki/Shrew "Shrew");
* *Les Nèg'marrons* − groups of men dressed in red [loincloths](/wiki/Loincloth "Loincloth"), bearing ripe tomatoes in their mouths while their bodies are smeared with grease or [molasses](/wiki/Molasses "Molasses"). They deliberately try to come in contact with spectators, soiling their clothes;
* *Les makoumés* − [cross-dressing](/wiki/Cross-dressing "Cross-dressing") men (out of the Carnival context, *makoumé* is a pejorative term for a [homosexual](/wiki/Homosexual "Homosexual"));
* *Soussouris* (the bat) − a character dressed in a winged [leotard](/wiki/Leotard "Leotard") from head to foot, usually black in colour. Traditionally malevolent, this character is liable to chase spectators and "sting" them.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Toulou.jpg/250px-Toulou.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toulou.jpg)

Four touloulous

A uniquely Creole tradition are the *touloulous*. These women wear decorative gowns, gloves, masks, and headdresses that cover them completely, making them unrecognisable, even to the colour of their skin. On Friday and Saturday nights of Carnival, touloulou balls are held in so-called "universities", large dance halls that open only at Carnival time. Touloulous get in free, and are even given [condoms](/wiki/Condom "Condom") in the interest of the sexual health of the community. Men attend the balls, but they pay admittance and are not disguised. The touloulous pick their dance partners, who may not refuse. The setup is designed to make it easy for a woman to create a temporary liaison with a man in total anonymity. Undisguised women are not welcomed. By tradition, if such a woman gets up to dance, the orchestra stops playing. Alcohol is served at bars – the disguised women whisper to the men "touloulou thirsty", at which a round of drinks is expected, to be drunk through a straw protect their anonymity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In more modern times, Guyanais men have attempted to turn the tables by staging *soirées tololo*, in which it is the men who, in disguise, seek partners from undisguised women bystanders.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The final four days of Carnival follow a rigid schedule, and no work is done:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Sunday − The Grand Parade, in which the groups compete.
* Monday − Marriage burlesque, with men dressed as brides and women as grooms.
* Tuesday − Red Devil Day in which everyone wears red or black.
* (Ash) Wednesday − Dress is black and white only, for the grand ceremony of burning the effigy of Vaval, King Carnival.

#### Guatemala

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Guatemala")]

The [Mazatenango](/wiki/Mazatenango "Mazatenango") carnival is a two- or three-day celebration that has been celebrated in this city for more than a century. Though secular in nature, it takes place immediately before Christian [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") begins. Early celebrations included bull fights, and modern celebrations include the wearing of masks and costumes, the selection of an Ugly King and the Queen of the Carnival, dances, and a variety of games.[[99]](#cite_note-Aldana_2023_j728-99)[[100]](#cite_note-PL_2018_v755-100)

#### Haiti

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Haiti")]

Main article: [Haitian Carnival](/wiki/Haitian_Carnival "Haitian Carnival")

Carnival in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") started in 1804 in the capital [Port-au-Prince](/wiki/Port-au-Prince "Port-au-Prince") after the [declaration of independence](/wiki/Haitian_Declaration_of_Independence "Haitian Declaration of Independence"). The Port-au-Prince Carnival is one of the largest in North America. It is known as Kanaval in the [Creole language](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language"). It starts in January, known as "Pre-Kanaval", while the main carnival activities begin in February. In July 2012, Haiti had another carnival called Kanaval de Fleur. Beautiful costumes, floats, [Rara festival](/wiki/Rara_festival "Rara festival") parades,[[101]](#cite_note-101) masks, foods, and popular [rasin](/wiki/Rasin "Rasin") music (such as [Boukman Eksperyans](/wiki/Boukman_Eksperyans "Boukman Eksperyans"), Foula Vodoule, Tokay, Boukan Ginen, and Eritaj) and [kompa](/wiki/Compas "Compas") bands (such as T-Vice, Djakout No. 1, [Sweet Micky](/wiki/Sweet_Micky "Sweet Micky"), Kreyòl La, D.P. Express, Mizik Mizik, Ram, T-Micky, Carimi, Djakout Mizik, and Scorpio Fever) play for dancers in the streets of the plaza of Champ-de-Mars, Port-au-Prince. A song competition takes place.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Other places in Haiti celebrate carnival, including [Jacmel](/wiki/Jacmel "Jacmel") and [Aux Cayes](/wiki/Aux_Cayes "Aux Cayes"). In 2013, Kanaval was celebrated in Okap ([Cap-Haïtien](/wiki/Cap-Ha%C3%AFtien "Cap-Haïtien")).[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Carnival finishes on Ash Wednesday, followed by rara festival, another parading musical tradition known mainly in Haiti and in the Dominican Republic. This festival emphasises religion. Songs are composed each year, and bands play bamboo tubes (*vaksin*) and homemade horns (*konèt*). Rara is also performed in [Prospect](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)") and [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in summertime New York.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

#### Honduras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Honduras")]

In [La Ceiba](/wiki/La_Ceiba "La Ceiba") in [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), Carnival is held on the third or fourth Saturday of every May to commemorate [San Isidro](/wiki/Isidore_the_Laborer "Isidore the Laborer").[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) It is the largest Carnival celebration in [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America").[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Main article: [Carnival in Mexico](/wiki/Carnival_in_Mexico "Carnival in Mexico")

In [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), *Carnaval* is celebrated in about 225 cities and towns. The largest are in [Mazatlán](/wiki/Mazatl%C3%A1n "Mazatlán") and the city of [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz_(city) "Veracruz (city)"), with others in [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") and [Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n "Yucatán"). The larger city Carnavals employ costumes, elected queens, and parades with floats, but Carnaval celebrations in smaller and rural areas vary widely depending on the level of European influence during Mexico's colonial period. The largest of these is in [Huejotzingo](/wiki/Huejotzingo "Huejotzingo"), [Puebla](/wiki/Puebla "Puebla"), where most townspeople take part in mock combat with rifles shooting blanks, roughly based on the [Battle of Puebla](/wiki/Battle_of_Puebla "Battle of Puebla"). Other important states with local traditions include [Morelos](/wiki/Morelos "Morelos"), [Oaxaca](/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca"), [Tlaxcala](/wiki/Tlaxcala "Tlaxcala"), and [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas").[[108]](#cite_note-108) Carnaval of [Campeche](/wiki/Campeche "Campeche") goes back 400 years, to 1582.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

#### Nicaragua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Nicaragua")]

On the Caribbean coast of [Bluefields](/wiki/Bluefields "Bluefields"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), Carnival is better known as "Palo de Mayo" (or Mayo Ya!) and is celebrated every day of May.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

In [Managua](/wiki/Managua "Managua"), it is celebrated for two days. There it is named *Alegria por la vida* ("Joy for Life") and features a different theme each year. Another festival in Managua celebrates patron saint Domingo de Guzman and lasts ten days.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

#### Panama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Panama")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Calle_Arriba_versus_Calle_Abajo_05.jpg/250px-Calle_Arriba_versus_Calle_Abajo_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calle_Arriba_versus_Calle_Abajo_05.jpg)

Two Queens, representing Calle Arriba and Calle Abajo, standing on a float

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Carnaval20150215.jpeg/250px-Carnaval20150215.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Carnaval20150215.jpeg)

A culeco

Traditionally beginning on Friday and ending on the Tuesday before Ash Wednesday, "los Carnavales", as [Panamanians](/wiki/Panama "Panama") refer to the days of Carnival, are celebrated across the country. Carnival Week is especially popular in the sleepy town of [Las Tablas](/wiki/Las_Tablas,_Los_Santos "Las Tablas, Los Santos"). The population multiplies because of the opulent Carnival celebrations. Carnival celebrations in [Panama City](/wiki/Panama_City "Panama City") and almost all of the [Azuero Peninsula](/wiki/Azuero_Peninsula "Azuero Peninsula") are popular tourist attractions. Penonomé features a parade on the Rio Zarati as a unique Carnival event.

The Panamanian Carnival is also popular because of the concerts featuring popular artists in the most visited areas. Concerts are often carried out during the night, and continue until the next morning.

Carnival Week is a national holiday in Panama, with most businesses and government offices remaining closed during its duration, and with most Panamanians opting to go to the country's rural areas to participate on the Carnivals and visit their relatives.

Carnivals in Panama also feature large repurposed fuel trucks that are used for soaking attendees through the use of firehoses that are controlled and directed by one or more people that stand in a platform that is mounted on top of the truck. This is known as "culecos" or "los culecos". Trucks get their water from nearby, government-approved rivers, and the water is tested for cleanliness before use. Culecos are often performed from 10 am to 3 pm, when the sun is at its brightest. Children and pregnant women are banned from participating in the culecos, and the trucks are always sponsored by a well-known Panamanian company or brand. The culecos are also often accompanied by reggaeton concerts.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)

The open consumption of large amounts of cold, [low-alcohol beer](/wiki/Low-alcohol_beer "Low-alcohol beer") or [Smirnoff](/wiki/Smirnoff "Smirnoff"), stored in ice-filled coolers, is common among attendees. Just like in Rio de Janeiro, some carnivals also feature floats, but they may have young women with elaborate costumes that stand as the "Queens" of "Calle Arriba" and "Calle Abajo", representing rich and working-class people, respectively. The queens are chosen through a contest and announced on October of the previous year, and are replaced every year. The queens are introduced on the first carnival day, and are always accompanied by a music band, who are present whenever the queens are present. Fireworks are launched on the last carnival night, to signal the end of the carnival.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

#### Peru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Peru")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Carnaval_de_juliaca.jpg/250px-Carnaval_de_juliaca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnaval_de_juliaca.jpg)

Morenada dance, in the Carnival of [Juliaca](/wiki/Juliaca "Juliaca"), Peru

##### Cajamarca

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Cajamarca")]

The town of [Cajamarca](/wiki/Cajamarca "Cajamarca") is considered the capital of Carnival in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"). Local residents of all ages dance around the *unsha*, or *yunsa*, a tree adorned with ribbons, balloons, toys, fruits, bottles of liquor, and other prizes.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

At a certain point, the *Mayordomo* (governor of the feast) walks into the circle. The governor chooses a partner to go to the *unsha*, which they attempt to cut down by striking it three times with a [machete](/wiki/Machete "Machete"). The machete is passed from couple to couple as each strikes the tree three times. When the unsha finally falls, the crowd rushes to grab the prizes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The person who successfully brings down the unsha becomes the following year's governor.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

##### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Crime")]

While generally peaceful, there have been issues with people using Carnival as a pretext for crime, particularly robbery or vandalism, especially in certain areas of Lima.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

#### Trinidad and Tobago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Trinidad and Tobago")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Orange_Carnival_Masqueraders_in_Trinidad.jpg/250px-Orange_Carnival_Masqueraders_in_Trinidad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orange_Carnival_Masqueraders_in_Trinidad.jpg)
