Masqueraders chipping on Carnival Tuesday in [Port of Spain](/wiki/Port_of_Spain "Port of Spain") during [Trinidad and Tobago Carnival](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago_Carnival "Trinidad and Tobago Carnival")

Main article: [Trinidad and Tobago Carnival](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago_Carnival "Trinidad and Tobago Carnival")

In [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), Carnival lasts months and culminates in large celebrations on the three days before Ash Wednesday with Dimanche Gras, [J'ouvert](/wiki/J%27ouvert "J'ouvert"), and Mas (masquerade). Tobago's celebration culminates on Monday and Tuesday on a much smaller scale, however Tobago hosted its inaugural standalone carnival on October 28–30, 2022[[120]](#cite_note-120) Carnival combines costumes, dance, music, competitions, rum, and partying (fete-ing). Music styles include soca, [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"), [rapso](/wiki/Rapso "Rapso"), and more recently [chutney](/wiki/Chutney_music "Chutney music") and [chutney soca](/wiki/Chutney_soca "Chutney soca").[[121]](#cite_note-121)

The annual Carnival [steel pan](/wiki/Steel_pan "Steel pan") competition known as the National Panorama competition holds the finals on the Saturday before the main event. Pan players compete in categories such as "Conventional Steel Band" or "Single Pan Band" by performing renditions of the year's calypsos.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

"Dimanche Gras" takes place on the Sunday night before Ash Wednesday. Here the [Calypso Monarch](/wiki/Calypso_Monarch "Calypso Monarch") is chosen (after competition) and prize money and a vehicle awarded. The King and Queen of the bands are crowned, where each band parades costumes for two days and submits a king and queen, from which an overall winner is chosen. These usually involve huge, complex, beautiful well-crafted costumes, that includes 'wire-bending'.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

J'ouvert, or "Dirty Mas", takes place before dawn on the Monday (known as Carnival Monday) before Ash Wednesday. It means "opening of the day". Revelers dress in costumes embodying puns on current affairs, especially political and social events. "Clean Mud" (clay mud), oil paint and body paint are familiar during J'ouvert. A common character is "Jab-jabs" (devils, blue, black, or red) complete with pitchfork, pointed horns and tails. A King and Queen of J'ouvert are chosen, based on their witty political/social messages.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Carnival_Costume_in_Trinidad.jpg/250px-Carnival_Costume_in_Trinidad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnival_Costume_in_Trinidad.jpg)

The Carnival King costume for a particular band

Carnival Monday involves the parade of the mas bands. Revelers wear only parts of their costumes, more for fun than display or competition. Monday Night Mas is popular in most towns and especially the capital, where smaller bands compete. There is also the "Bomb Competition", a smaller-scaled judging of steel bands.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Carnival Tuesday hosts the main events. Full costume is worn, complete with make-up and body paint/adornment. Usually "Mas Boots" that complement the costumes are worn. Each band has their costume presentation based on a particular theme, and contains various sections (some consisting of thousands of revelers) that reflect these themes. The street parade and band costume competition take place. The mas bands eventually converge on the Queen's Park Savannah to pass on "The Stage" for judging. The singer of the most played song is crowned Road March King or Queen, earning prize money and usually a vehicle.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This parading and revelry goes on until Tuesday midnight. Ash Wednesday itself, while not an official holiday, sends flocks to local beaches. The most popular are [Maracas Beach](/wiki/Maracas_Beach "Maracas Beach") and [Manzanilla Beach](/wiki/Manzanilla_Beach,_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Manzanilla Beach, Trinidad and Tobago"), where huge beach parties take place on Ash Wednesday.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: United States")]

Main articles: [Mardi Gras in the United States](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_the_United_States "Mardi Gras in the United States"); [Mardi Gras in Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_Mobile,_Alabama "Mardi Gras in Mobile, Alabama"); [Mardi Gras in New Orleans](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_New_Orleans "Mardi Gras in New Orleans"); [Carnaval de Ponce](/wiki/Carnaval_de_Ponce "Carnaval de Ponce"); and [Courir de Mardi Gras](/wiki/Courir_de_Mardi_Gras "Courir de Mardi Gras")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/StAnne06Marigny29.jpg/250px-StAnne06Marigny29.jpg)](/wiki/File:StAnne06Marigny29.jpg)

Revelers on [Frenchmen Street](/wiki/Frenchmen_Street "Frenchmen Street"), in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), USA

Carnival celebrations, usually referred to as [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras") ("Fat Tuesday" in French), were first celebrated in the [Gulf Coast](/wiki/Gulf_Coast_of_the_United_States "Gulf Coast of the United States") area, but now occur in many states.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Customs originated in the onetime [French colonial](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") capitals of [Mobile](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_Mobile,_Alabama "Mardi Gras in Mobile, Alabama") (now in [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama")), [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") ([Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana")), and [Biloxi](/wiki/Biloxi,_Mississippi "Biloxi, Mississippi") ([Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi")), all of which have celebrated for many years with street parades and masquerade balls. Other major American cities with celebrations include [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.");[[127]](#cite_note-127) [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri;[[128]](#cite_note-128) [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco")[[129]](#cite_note-129) and [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"),[[130]](#cite_note-130) California; [Galveston, Texas](/wiki/Galveston,_Texas "Galveston, Texas");[[131]](#cite_note-131) and [Pensacola](/wiki/Pensacola,_Florida "Pensacola, Florida"),[[132]](#cite_note-132) [Tampa](/wiki/Tampa,_Florida "Tampa, Florida"),[[133]](#cite_note-133)[Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida"),[[134]](#cite_note-134) and [Miami](/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida")[[135]](#cite_note-135) in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida").

The most widely known, elaborate, and popular U.S. events are in [New Orleans](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_New_Orleans "Mardi Gras in New Orleans"), where [krewes](/wiki/Krewe "Krewe") organize parades, balls, and other activities starting with Phunny Phorty Phellows streetcar parade on [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)") and ending with the closing of Bourbon Street at midnight on Fat Tuesday.[[136]](#cite_note-136) It is often called "the greatest free party on earth".[[137]](#cite_note-137) Many other Louisiana cities such as [Lake Charles](/wiki/Lake_Charles,_Louisiana "Lake Charles, Louisiana"), [Baton Rouge](/wiki/Baton_Rouge,_Louisiana "Baton Rouge, Louisiana"), [Shreveport](/wiki/Shreveport,_Louisiana "Shreveport, Louisiana"), [Lafayette](/wiki/Lafayette,_Louisiana "Lafayette, Louisiana"), [Mamou](/wiki/Mamou,_Louisiana "Mamou, Louisiana"), [Houma](/wiki/Houma,_Louisiana "Houma, Louisiana"), and [Thibodaux](/wiki/Thibodaux,_Louisiana "Thibodaux, Louisiana"), most of which were under French control at one time or another, also hold Carnival celebrations.[[138]](#cite_note-138) On the prairie country northwest of Lafayette, Louisiana, the [Cajuns](/wiki/Cajun "Cajun") celebrate the traditional [Courir de Mardi Gras](/wiki/Courir_de_Mardi_Gras "Courir de Mardi Gras"), which has its roots in celebrations from rural Medieval France.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

In [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), the most popular festivals are the Carnaval de [Loíza](/wiki/Lo%C3%ADza,_Puerto_Rico "Loíza, Puerto Rico") and [Carnaval de Ponce](/wiki/Carnaval_de_Ponce "Carnaval de Ponce"). The Carnaval de Ponce (officially "Carnaval Ponceño") is celebrated annually in [Ponce](/wiki/Ponce,_Puerto_Rico "Ponce, Puerto Rico"). The celebration lasts one week and ends on the day before Ash Wednesday. It is one of the oldest carnivals of the Western Hemisphere, dating to 1858.[[140]](#cite_note-140) Some authorities trace the Ponce Carnaval to the eighteenth century.[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)

In New York City, a Caribbean Carnival known as the [West Indian Day Parade](/wiki/West_Indian_Day_Parade "West Indian Day Parade") is held in [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") on [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day"). Founded by immigrants from Trinidad, the event was initially held at the beginning of Lent, but was later moved to Labor Day in order to allow an outdoor event in the summer.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

Starting in 2013, the Slovenian-American community located in the St. Clair-Superior neighborhood of [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") began hosting a local version of [Kurentovanje](/wiki/Kurentovanje "Kurentovanje"), the Carnival event held in the city of [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj"), [Slovenia](#Slovenia).[[144]](#cite_note-:0-144) The event is conducted on the Saturday prior to Ash Wednesday.[[144]](#cite_note-:0-144)

#### Uruguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Uruguay")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Candombe1870-Uruguay.jpg/250px-Candombe1870-Uruguay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Candombe1870-Uruguay.jpg)

Afro-Uruguayans gathering for a [Candombe](/wiki/Candombe "Candombe") celebration, c. 1870

The Carnival in Uruguay lasts more than 40 days, generally beginning towards the end of January and running through mid March. Celebrations in [Montevideo](/wiki/Montevideo "Montevideo") are the largest. The festival is performed in the European parade style with elements from [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") and [Angolan](/wiki/Angola "Angola") [Benguela](/wiki/Benguela "Benguela") cultures imported with slaves in colonial times. The main attractions of Uruguayan Carnival include two colorful parades called *Desfile de Carnaval* (Carnival Parade) and *Desfile de Llamadas* (Calls Parade, a [candombe](/wiki/Candombe "Candombe")-summoning parade).[[145]](#cite_note-145)

During the celebration, theaters called *tablados* are built in many places throughout the cities, especially in Montevideo.[[146]](#cite_note-146) Traditionally formed by men and now starting to be open to women, the different Carnival groups ([Murgas](/wiki/Murga "Murga"), Lubolos, or Parodistas) perform a kind of popular opera at the *tablados*, singing and dancing songs that generally relate to the social and political situation. The 'Calls' groups, basically formed by drummers playing the tamboril, perform candombe rhythmic figures. The carnival in Uruguay have [escolas de samba](/wiki/Escolas_de_samba "Escolas de samba") too, and the biggest samba parades are in [Artigas](/wiki/Artigas,_Uruguay "Artigas, Uruguay") and in [Montevideo](/wiki/Montevideo "Montevideo"). Revelers wear their festival clothing. [Vedettes](/wiki/Vedette_(cabaret) "Vedette (cabaret)"), exuberant women wearing elegant, bright dresses, are also part of the parades.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Each group has its own theme.

European [archetypes](/wiki/Archetype "Archetype") ([Pierrot](/wiki/Pierrot "Pierrot"), [Harlequin](/wiki/Harlequin "Harlequin"), and [Columbina](/wiki/Columbina "Columbina")) merge with African ancestral elements (the [Old Mother](/wiki/Matriarchy "Matriarchy") or *Mama Vieja*, the [Medicine Man](/wiki/Medicine_Man "Medicine Man") or *Gramillero* and the [Magician](/wiki/Magician_(paranormal) "Magician (paranormal)") or *Escobero*) in the festival.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

#### Venezuela

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Venezuela")]

Carnival in [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") covers two days, 40 days before Easter. It is a time when youth in many rural towns have water fights (including the use of water balloons and water guns). Any pedestrian risks getting soaked. Coastal towns and provinces celebrate Carnival more fervently than elsewhere in the country. Venezuelans regard Carnival about the same way they regard Christmas and Semana Santa ([Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"); the week before Easter Sunday) when they take the opportunity to visit their families.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: India")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Carnival_Goa_circa_1980s_or_1990s.jpg/250px-Carnival_Goa_circa_1980s_or_1990s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnival_Goa_circa_1980s_or_1990s.jpg)

Panaji locals participating at the [Goan Carnival](/wiki/Carnival_in_Goa "Carnival in Goa"), India, late 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Goa_Carnival.jpg/250px-Goa_Carnival.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goa_Carnival.jpg)

Foreign tourist revellers at the modern Goan Carnival, India, 2005

In India, Carnival is celebrated only in the state of [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") and was originally a local Roman Catholic tradition known as **Intruz** which means "swindler" in [Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language") while *Entrudo* is the appropriate word in [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") for "Carnival". The present commercial version of the Goan carnival ([King Momo](/wiki/King_Momo "King Momo"), floats, etc.) was created based on the [Rio Carnival](/wiki/Rio_Carnival "Rio Carnival") only in the 1960s as a means of attracting tourism.[[149]](#cite_note-149) The largest celebration takes place in the capital [Panaji](/wiki/Panaji "Panaji"). The commercial Carnival festivities occur during the three days and nights preceding Ash Wednesday. Sixtus Eric Dias from Candolim was the King Momo for the Carnival 2021. All-night parades occur throughout the state with bands, dances, and floats. Grand balls are held in the evenings.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

#### Indonesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Indonesia")]

In [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), the word "carnival" or *karnaval* is not related to pre-[Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") festivities, but more to festivals in general, especially those with processions and extravagant costumes. One of the largest carnivals in Indonesia is the [Solo Batik Carnival](/wiki/Solo_Batik_Carnival "Solo Batik Carnival"), held in [Solo](/wiki/Surakarta "Surakarta"), [Central Java](/wiki/Central_Java "Central Java"). The Jember Fashion Carnaval is held in [Jember](/wiki/Jember "Jember"), [East Java](/wiki/East_Java "East Java").[[151]](#cite_note-151)

The Roman Catholic community of [Kupang](/wiki/Kupang "Kupang"), [East Nusa Tenggara](/wiki/East_Nusa_Tenggara "East Nusa Tenggara"), held an Easter procession in form of an Easter Carnival called *Pawai Paskah Kupang*.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

#### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main article: [Manila Carnival](/wiki/Manila_Carnival "Manila Carnival")

See also: [List of festivals in the Philippines](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_the_Philippines "List of festivals in the Philippines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/09288jfEscolta_Museum_Exhibit_Hall_Edificio_Calvo_Binondo%2C_Manilafvf_03.jpg/250px-09288jfEscolta_Museum_Exhibit_Hall_Edificio_Calvo_Binondo%2C_Manilafvf_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:09288jfEscolta_Museum_Exhibit_Hall_Edificio_Calvo_Binondo,_Manilafvf_03.jpg)

Posters and flyers of the [Manila Carnival](/wiki/Manila_Carnival "Manila Carnival") as well as photos of crowned Carnival Queens (c. 1920s-1930s), from the [Escolta Museum](/wiki/Calvo_Building "Calvo Building")

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), the [Manila Carnival](/wiki/Manila_Carnival "Manila Carnival") ([Philippine Spanish](/wiki/Philippine_Spanish "Philippine Spanish"): *Carnaval de Manila*) was formerly a major annual event in [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila") during the early [American colonial period](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(1898%E2%80%931946) "History of the Philippines (1898–1946)") up to the time before the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). It was organized by the American colonial administration to showcase the economic development of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). The highlight of the event is the crowning of the Carnival Queens, which is the precursor of later national pageants like [Miss Philippines](/wiki/Miss_Philippines "Miss Philippines").[[153]](#cite_note-mcbs-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-Clutario-155) It is also notable for founding and hosting the first [Far Eastern Championship Games](/wiki/Far_Eastern_Championship_Games "Far Eastern Championship Games") (then known as the "First Oriental Olympic Games") which became the precursor of the modern [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games").[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Carnival_at_Manila_postcard.jpg/250px-Carnival_at_Manila_postcard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnival_at_Manila_postcard.jpg)

Postcard from the [Manila Carnival](/wiki/Manila_Carnival "Manila Carnival") (c. 1920s)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Masskara_Festival_Street_Dance_22.jpg/250px-Masskara_Festival_Street_Dance_22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Masskara_Festival_Street_Dance_22.jpg)

Parade contingents in the 2024 [Masskara Festival](/wiki/Masskara_Festival "Masskara Festival") of [Bacolod](/wiki/Bacolod "Bacolod"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Sinulog_Festival_%282023%29_contingents_in_street_dance_10.jpg/250px-Sinulog_Festival_%282023%29_contingents_in_street_dance_10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sinulog_Festival_(2023)_contingents_in_street_dance_10.jpg)

Parade contingents in the 2023 [Sinulog Festival](/wiki/Sinulog_Festival "Sinulog Festival") of [Cebu](/wiki/Cebu_City "Cebu City"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")

The carnival lasted around two weeks in which a variety of shows were presented like [circus](/wiki/Circus "Circus"), [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville"), [slapstick](/wiki/Slapstick "Slapstick") comedies, and [theater](/wiki/Theater "Theater").[[158]](#cite_note-158) Five parades were also held, an opening parade, a military parade, a school parade, a business and industry parade (which included foreign participants), and the floral parade (which featured the Carnival Queen winner).[[159]](#cite_note-159)

The carnival was first held in February 1908 and was initially only a city celebration. It expanded to a national scope by 1912,[[153]](#cite_note-mcbs-153) the year that it was held concurrently with the Philippine Exposition.[[160]](#cite_note-160) It was discontinued in 1939, due to the outbreak of the Second World War. It was also not held during the [Japanese occupation of the Philippines](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_the_Philippines "Japanese occupation of the Philippines") (1941 to 1944). After [Philippine independence](/wiki/Philippine_independence "Philippine independence") in 1946, it was never renewed.[[153]](#cite_note-mcbs-153)[[161]](#cite_note-161)

In the modern Philippines, thousands of annual festivals and [fiestas](/wiki/Patronal_festival "Patronal festival") throughout the islands feature public celebrations and street parade competitions with elaborate costumes similar to the carnival. However, they are not tied to the Lenten period and are instead celebrations of a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint"), of the local culture, or both.

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Albania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Albania")]

Carnivals have begun to be celebrated in Korça before 1940, a period in which cultural life in this city has been varied. Although a pagan holiday, the Carnival was later celebrated on Feast Day in February. In addition to the many carnival-wearing individuals, there were bands with guitars, bows, and butaphoric[*[check spelling](/wiki/Template:Typo_help_inline "Template:Typo help inline")*] masks such as animal heads and humans. The Korça Carnival took a big hit after the creation of cultural societies such as the "Korça Youth". During this period carnivals were accompanied by mandolins, guitars and humorous songs. Carnival celebrations were discontinued after 1960, to resume in other social conditions after 1990. Korça is one of the first cities to revive the Carnival tradition by establishing the Carnival Association in 1992 as part of the National Carnival Association of Albania. On 10 April 1994, the first International Carnival Festival in Albania was organized in Korça. The following year, the second International Carnival Festival is even larger. Since this year and until 2008, the Korca Carnival group has been represented at a number of international festivals organized in various European countries.

#### Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Belgium")]

Many parts of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") celebrate Carnival, typically with costume parades, partying and fireworks. These areas include the province of Limburg with its cities [Maasmechelen](/wiki/Maasmechelen "Maasmechelen"), [Maaseik](/wiki/Maaseik "Maaseik") and Lanaken along the river Meuse, the cities of [Aalst](/wiki/Aalst,_Belgium "Aalst, Belgium"), [Ninove](/wiki/Ninove "Ninove"), [Binche](/wiki/Binche "Binche"), [Eupen](/wiki/Eupen "Eupen"), [Halle](/wiki/Halle,_Belgium "Halle, Belgium"), [Heist](/wiki/Heist-aan-Zee "Heist-aan-Zee"), [Kelmis](/wiki/Kelmis "Kelmis"), [Malmedy](/wiki/Malmedy "Malmedy"), and [Stavelot](/wiki/Stavelot "Stavelot").[[162]](#cite_note-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Binche_-_Les_Gilles.jpg/250px-Binche_-_Les_Gilles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Binche_-_Les_Gilles.jpg)

The [Gilles](/wiki/Gilles "Gilles") at the [Carnival of Binche](/wiki/Carnival_of_Binche "Carnival of Binche"), in [Binche](/wiki/Binche "Binche"), Belgium

The [Carnival of Binche](/wiki/Carnival_of_Binche "Carnival of Binche") dates at least to the 14th century. Parades are held over the three days before Lent; the most important participants are the [Gilles](/wiki/Gilles "Gilles"), who wear traditional costumes on Shrove Tuesday and throw [blood oranges](/wiki/Blood_orange "Blood orange") to the crowd.[[163]](#cite_note-163) In 2003, the Carnival of Binche was recognised as one of the [Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Masterpieces_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity").[[164]](#cite_note-164)

The [Carnival of Aalst](/wiki/Carnival_of_Aalst "Carnival of Aalst"), celebrated during the three days preceding Ash Wednesday, received the same recognition in 2010.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

Belgium's oldest parade is the [Carnival Parade of Maaseik](/wiki/Carnival_Parade_of_Maaseik "Carnival Parade of Maaseik"), also held on Laetare Sunday, which originated in 1865.[[166]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Un_petit_groupe_de_%22Longs_N%C3%A9s%22.jpg/250px-Un_petit_groupe_de_%22Longs_N%C3%A9s%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Un_petit_groupe_de_%22Longs_N%C3%A9s%22.jpg)

*Long-Né* and *Longuès-Brèsses* at the Carnival of Malmedy (*[Cwarmê](/wiki/Cwarm%C3%AA "Cwarmê")*), in [Malmedy](/wiki/Malmedy "Malmedy"), Belgium

The Carnival of [Malmedy](/wiki/Malmedy "Malmedy") is locally called *[Cwarmê](/wiki/Cwarm%C3%AA "Cwarmê")*. Even if Malmedy is located in the east Belgium, near the German-speaking area, the *Cwarmê* is a pure [Walloon](/wiki/Walloons "Walloons") and Latin carnival. The celebration takes place during the four days before Shrove Tuesday. The *Cwarmê* Sunday is the most important and interesting to see. All the old traditional costumes parade in the street. The *Cwarmê* is a "street carnival" and is not only a parade. People who are disguised pass through the crowd and perform a part of the traditional costume they wear. The famous traditional costumes at the *Cwarmê* of Malmedy are the *Haguète*, the *Longuès-Brèsses*, and the *Long-Né.*[[167]](#cite_note-167)

Some Belgian cities hold Carnivals during Lent. One of the best-known is [Stavelot](/wiki/Stavelot "Stavelot"), where the *Carnival de la Laetare* takes place on [Laetare Sunday](/wiki/Laetare_Sunday "Laetare Sunday"), the fourth Sunday of Lent. The participants include the *Blancs-Moussis*, who dress in white, carry long red noses and parade through town attacking bystanders with [confetti](/wiki/Confetti "Confetti") and dried [pig bladders](/wiki/Pig_bladder "Pig bladder"). The city of [Halle](/wiki/Halle,_Belgium#Events "Halle, Belgium") also celebrates on Laetare Sunday.

#### Bosnia and Herzegovina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Bosnia and Herzegovina")]

In [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), the [Croat](/wiki/Croat "Croat")-majority city of [Ljubuški](/wiki/Ljubu%C5%A1ki "Ljubuški") holds a traditional Carnival ([Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"): *Karneval*). Ljubuški is a member of the [Federation of European Carnival Cities](/wiki/Federation_of_European_Carnival_Cities "Federation of European Carnival Cities") (FECC).[[168]](#cite_note-168)

#### Croatia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Croatia")]

The most famous [Croatian](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") Carnival (Croatian: *karneval*, also called *maškare* or *fašnik*) is the [Rijeka Carnival](/wiki/Rijeka_Carnival "Rijeka Carnival"), during which the mayor of [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") hands over the keys to the city to the Carnival master (*meštar od karnevala*). The festival includes several events, culminating on the final Sunday in a masked procession. (A similar procession for children takes place on the previous weekend.)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Rijecki_karneval_140210_Halubajski_zvoncari_9.jpg/250px-Rijecki_karneval_140210_Halubajski_zvoncari_9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rijecki_karneval_140210_Halubajski_zvoncari_9.jpg)

[Halubajski zvončari](/wiki/Halubajski_zvon%C4%8Dari "Halubajski zvončari") at [Rijeka Carnival](/wiki/Rijeka_Carnival "Rijeka Carnival"), in [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka"), Croatia

Many towns in Croatia's [Kvarner](/wiki/Kvarner "Kvarner") region (and in other parts of the country, e.g. in [Međimurje County](/wiki/Me%C4%91imurje_County "Međimurje County") in [Northern Croatia](/wiki/Northern_Croatia "Northern Croatia")) observe the Carnival period, incorporating local traditions and celebrating local culture. Some of the towns and places are Grobnik, Permani, Kastav and many others places near Rijeka, then [Čakovec](/wiki/%C4%8Cakovec "Čakovec"), [Samobor](/wiki/Samobor "Samobor") etc. Just before the end of Carnival, every Kvarner town burns a [man-like doll](/wiki/Effigy "Effigy") called a "Pust", who is blamed for all the strife of the previous year. The [Zvončari](/wiki/Zvon%C4%8Dari "Zvončari"), or bell-ringers push away winter and all the bad things in the past year and calling spring, they wear bells and large head regalia representing their areas of origin (for example, those from Halubje wear regalia in the shape of animal heads). The traditional Carnival food is [fritule](/wiki/Fritule "Fritule"), a pastry. This festival can also be called Poklade.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Me%C4%91imurski_fa%C5%A1njak_2015._-_Svetomartinski_krampus.jpg/250px-Me%C4%91imurski_fa%C5%A1njak_2015._-_Svetomartinski_krampus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Me%C4%91imurski_fa%C5%A1njak_2015._-_Svetomartinski_krampus.jpg)

A [Krampus](/wiki/Krampus "Krampus") from [Međimurje County](/wiki/Me%C4%91imurje_County "Međimurje County"), northern Croatia

Masks are worn to many of the festivities, including concerts and parties. Children and teachers are commonly allowed to wear masks to school for a day, and also wear masks at school dances or while trick-or-treating. Carnivals also take place in summer. One of the most famous is the Senj Summer Carnival – first celebrated in 1968. The towns of [Cres](/wiki/Cres "Cres"), Pag, Novi Vinodolski, and Fužine also organise Summer Carnivals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Cyprus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Cyprus")]

Carnival has been celebrated in [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") for centuries. The tradition was likely established under [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") rule around the 16th century. It may have been influenced by Greek traditions, such as festivities for deities such as [Dionysus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus"). The celebration originally involved dressing in costumes and holding masked balls or visiting friends. In the twentieth century, it became an organized event held during the 10 days preceding Lent (according to the [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox "Greek Orthodox") calendar). The festival is celebrated almost exclusively in the city of [Limassol](/wiki/Limassol "Limassol").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Three main parades take place during Carnival. The first is held on the first day, during which the "Carnival King" (either a person in costume or an [effigy](/wiki/Effigy "Effigy")) rides through the city on his carriage. The second is held on the first Sunday of the festival, and the participants are mainly children. The third and largest takes place on the last day of Carnival and involves hundreds of people walking in costume along the town's longest avenue. The latter two parades are open to anyone who wishes to participate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Czech Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Czech Republic")]

See also: [Slavic carnival](/wiki/Slavic_carnival "Slavic carnival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Masopust_masks_in_Milevsku_%282013%29_63.JPG/250px-Masopust_masks_in_Milevsku_%282013%29_63.JPG)](/wiki/File:Masopust_masks_in_Milevsku_(2013)_63.JPG)

Masopust masks in [Milevsko](/wiki/Milevsko "Milevsko"), Czech Republic

In the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), the Masopust Festival takes place from [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") (*Den tří králů*) through Ash Wednesday (*Popeleční středa*). The word *masopust* translates literally from old Czech to mean "meat fast", and the festival often includes a pork feast. The tradition is most common in [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") but also occurs in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"). While practices vary, masks and costumes are present everywhere.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

#### Denmark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Denmark")]

Main article: [Fastelavn](/wiki/Fastelavn "Fastelavn")

Carnival in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") is called *Fastelavn*, and is held on the Sunday or Monday before Ash Wednesday. The holiday is sometimes described as a Nordic [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween"), with children dressing in costume and gathering treats for the *Fastelavn* feast. One popular custom is the *fastelavnsris*, a [switch](/wiki/Switch_(corporal_punishment) "Switch (corporal punishment)") that children use to [flog](/wiki/Flagellation "Flagellation") their parents to wake them up on Fastelavns Sunday.[[170]](#cite_note-170)

#### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Norway")]

In [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), students having seen [celebrations in Paris](/wiki/Paris_Carnival "Paris Carnival") introduced Carnival processions, masked balls, and Carnival balls to [Christiana](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") in the 1840s and 1850s. From 1863, the artist federation *Kunstnerforeningen* held annual Carnival balls in the old [Freemasons](/wiki/Freemasons "Freemasons") lodge, which inspired [Johan Svendsen](/wiki/Johan_Svendsen "Johan Svendsen")'s compositions *Norsk Kunstnerkarneval* and *Karneval in Paris*. The following year, Svendsen's *Festpolonaise* was written for the opening procession. [Edvard Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg") attended and wrote "Aus dem Karneval" (*Folkelivsbilleder* Op. 19). Since 1988, the student organization [Tårnseilerne](/w/index.php?title=T%C3%A5rnseilerne&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tårnseilerne (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C3%A5rnseilerne "no:Tårnseilerne")] has produced annual masquerade balls in Oslo, with masks, costumes, and processions after attending an opera performance. The Carnival season also includes *Fastelavens søndag* (with cream buns) and *fastelavensris* with decorated branches.[[171]](#cite_note-171)

#### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Finland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Samba_wagon_from_Imp%C3%A9rio_do_Papagaio_at_Helsinki_Samba_Carnaval_2016.jpg/250px-Samba_wagon_from_Imp%C3%A9rio_do_Papagaio_at_Helsinki_Samba_Carnaval_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samba_wagon_from_Imp%C3%A9rio_do_Papagaio_at_Helsinki_Samba_Carnaval_2016.jpg)

The 2016 [Helsinki Samba Carnaval](/wiki/Helsinki_Samba_Carnaval "Helsinki Samba Carnaval") in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland

The [Helsinki Samba Carnaval](/wiki/Helsinki_Samba_Carnaval "Helsinki Samba Carnaval"), modelled after the samba carnivals in Brazil, takes place every year in middle June. The carnival lacks a proper historical tradition and has instead mostly grown from experimenting.

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: France")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Carnaval_de_Paris_15_f%C3%A9vrier_2015_7.JPG/250px-Carnaval_de_Paris_15_f%C3%A9vrier_2015_7.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carnaval_de_Paris_15_f%C3%A9vrier_2015_7.JPG)

[Carnival in Paris](/wiki/Paris_Carnival "Paris Carnival"), France

Some major Carnivals of mainland [France](/wiki/France "France") are the [Nice Carnival](/wiki/Nice_Carnival "Nice Carnival"), the Dunkirk Carnival and the Limoux Carnival. The [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") Carnival was held as far back as 1294, and annually attracts over a million visitors during the two weeks preceding Lent.

Since 1604, a characteristic [masked Carnival](/wiki/Carnival_of_Limoux "Carnival of Limoux") is celebrated in [Limoux](/wiki/Limoux "Limoux").

The [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk") Carnival is among the greatest and most exuberant carnivals celebrated in Europe. Its traditions date back to the 17th century and are based on the *vischerbende* as fishermen went from one café to another accompanied by their relatives and friends just before departing to [Icelandic](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") fishing grounds.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

In the [French West Indies](/wiki/French_West_Indies "French West Indies"), it occurs between the Sunday of Epiphany and Ash Wednesday;[[173]](#cite_note-173) this dates back to the arrival of French colonists in the islands.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

#### Germany and Austria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Germany and Austria")]

Main articles: [Karneval in Germany and Austria](/wiki/Carnival_in_Germany,_Switzerland_and_Austria "Carnival in Germany, Switzerland and Austria") and [Fastnacht or Fasnacht in Germany, Switzerland, Alsace and Austria](/wiki/Swabian-Alemannic_Fastnacht "Swabian-Alemannic Fastnacht")

##### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Germany")]

The earliest written record of Carnival in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") was in 1296 in [Speyer](/wiki/History_of_Speyer "History of Speyer").[[175]](#cite_note-175) The first worldwide Carnival parade took place in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") in 1823.[[48]](#cite_note-Cologne1823-48) The most active Carnival week begins on the Thursday before Ash Wednesday, with parades during the weekend, and finishes the night before Ash Wednesday, with the main festivities occurring around *Rosenmontag* (Rose Monday). This time is also called the "Fifth Season". Shrove Tuesday, called *Fastnacht* or *Veilchendienstag*, is celebrated in some cities.

Parties feature self-made and more fanciful costumes and occasional masks. The parties become more exuberant as the weeks progress and peak after New Year, in January and February. The final Tuesday features all-night parties, dancing, hugging, and smooching. Some parties are for all, some for women only and some for children. *Fasnachtsküchle* (similar to *Kreppel* or donuts) are the traditional Fasching food and are baked or fried.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In Germany, *Rheinischer Karnival* and *Schwäbische Fastnacht* are distinct; first is less formal and more political, second is much more traditional.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

##### "Rhenish Carnival" (Rheinischer Karneval, Fasnacht, Fasnet, Fastabend, Fastelovend, Fasteleer, Fasching)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: \"Rhenish Carnival\" (Rheinischer Karneval, Fasnacht, Fasnet, Fastabend, Fastelovend, Fasteleer, Fasching)")]

The "Rheinische" Carnival is held in the west of Germany, mainly in the states of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") and [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate"), but also in [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") (including [Upper Hesse](/wiki/Upper_Hesse "Upper Hesse")). Similar forms of the festival occur in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), and other states. Some cities are more famous for celebrations such as parades and costume balls. The [Cologne Carnival](/wiki/Cologne_Carnival "Cologne Carnival"), as well as those in [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"), [Eschweiler](/wiki/Eschweiler "Eschweiler") and [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf"), are the largest and most famous. Other cities have their own, often less well-known celebrations, parades, and parties, such as [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), [Worms am Rhein](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany"), [Speyer](/wiki/Speyer "Speyer"), [Kaiserslautern](/wiki/Kaiserslautern "Kaiserslautern"), [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt"), [Duisburg](/wiki/Duisburg "Duisburg"), [Dortmund](/wiki/Dortmund "Dortmund"), [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen"), [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"), [Münster](/wiki/M%C3%BCnster "Münster"), [Krefeld](/wiki/Krefeld "Krefeld"), [Ludwigshafen](/wiki/Ludwigshafen "Ludwigshafen"), [Mönchengladbach](/wiki/M%C3%B6nchengladbach "Mönchengladbach"), [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg"), [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), and [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"). The biggest German carnival club is located in the relatively small town of [Dieburg](/wiki/Dieburg "Dieburg") in South Hesse. Festivities start as early as 11 November at 11:11am for some carnival organizations, but the main events usually take place after the Christmas season.[[177]](#cite_note-177) On Carnival Thursday (called "Old Women Day" or "The Women's Day" in commemoration of an 1824 revolt by washer-women), women storm city halls, cut men's ties, and are allowed to kiss any passing man. Special acrobatic show dances in mock uniforms are a traditional contribution to most festive balls. They may or may not have been a source of inspiration to American cheerleading. The Fasching parades and floats make fun of individual politicians and other public figures. Many speeches do the same.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

Traditions often also include the "Faschingssitzung" – a sit-only party with dancing and singing presentations, and often many speeches given that humorously criticize politics.

##### "Swabian-Alemannic" Carnival (*Schwäbische Fastnacht*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: \"Swabian-Alemannic\" Carnival (Schwäbische Fastnacht)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/VSAN_TT_2014_Sa17.jpg/250px-VSAN_TT_2014_Sa17.jpg)](/wiki/File:VSAN_TT_2014_Sa17.jpg)

Reitenderle, der Grundholde, Hudelmale, Schnarragagges; popular Fasnet characters from [Kisslegg](/wiki/Kisslegg "Kisslegg") im Allgäu, [Swabia](/wiki/Swabia "Swabia"), Germany

The [Swabian-Alemannic Fastnacht](/wiki/Swabian-Alemannic_Fastnacht "Swabian-Alemannic Fastnacht"), known as *Schwäbische Fastnacht*, takes place in [Baden](/wiki/Baden "Baden"), [Swabia](/wiki/Swabia "Swabia"), the [Allgäu](/wiki/Allg%C3%A4u "Allgäu"), [Alsace](/wiki/Alsace "Alsace"), and [Vorarlberg](/wiki/Vorarlberg "Vorarlberg") (western [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria")). During the pagan era, it represented the time of year when the reign of the grim winter spirits is over, and these spirits are hunted and expelled. It then adapted to Catholicism. The first official record of Karneval, Fasching or Fastnacht in Germany dates to 1296.[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180) Often the costumes and masks on parades strictly follow traditional designs and represent specific historical characters, public figures – or specific daemons.

#### Switzerland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Switzerland")]

##### Swiss Fasnacht

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Swiss Fasnacht")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Monstercorso_2009.JPG/250px-Monstercorso_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Monstercorso_2009.JPG)

*Monstercorso* on [*Güdisdienstag's*](/wiki/Fat_Tuesday "Fat Tuesday") evening in [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne"), Switzerland

In [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), *Fasnacht* takes place in the Catholic [cantons of Switzerland](/wiki/Cantons_of_Switzerland "Cantons of Switzerland"), e.g. in [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne#events "Lucerne") (*Lozärner Fasnacht*), but also in Protestant [Basel](/wiki/Carnival_of_Basel "Carnival of Basel"). However, the *Basler Fasnacht* begins on the Monday *after* Ash Wednesday. Both began in the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"). Smaller Fasnacht festivities take place across German Switzerland, e.g. in [Bern](/wiki/Carnival_in_Bern "Carnival in Bern") and [Olten](/wiki/Olten "Olten"), or in the eastern part ([Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich"), [St. Gallen](/wiki/St._Gallen "St. Gallen"), [Appenzell](/wiki/Appenzell "Appenzell")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Greece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Greece")]

In [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), Carnival is known as *Apókries* (Απόκριες, lit. '[goodbye] to meat'), and officially begins with the "Opening of the [Triodion](/wiki/Triodion "Triodion")", the liturgical book used by the [Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") from then until [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").[[181]](#cite_note-HR-MFA-181) Apokries is made up of three themed weeks of celebration known as *Prophoní* (Προφωνή, 'preannouncement week'), *Kreatiní* (Κρεατινή, 'meat week'), and *Tiriní* (Τυρινή, 'cheese week'). One of the season's high points during Kreatini is *[Tsiknopémpti](/wiki/Tsiknopempti "Tsiknopempti")* (lit. 'Smoky-Thursday'), when celebrants throw large outdoor parties and roast huge amounts of meat; the ritual is repeated the following Sunday, after which point meat is forbidden until Easter. The following week, Tirini, is marked by similar festivities revolving around the consumption of cheese, eggs, and dairy and culminates with a "Cheese Sunday". [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"), and its restrictive fasting rules, begins in earnest the next day on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"). Throughout the Carnival season, festivals, parades, and balls are held all over the country. Many people disguise themselves as *maskarádes* ("masqueraders") and engage in pranks and revelry throughout the season.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Kingcarnival.jpg/250px-Kingcarnival.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kingcarnival.jpg)

The float of the King Carnival parading in [Patras](/wiki/Patras "Patras"), Greece

[Patras](/wiki/Patras "Patras") holds the largest annual Carnival in Greece, and one of the largest in the world. The famous [Patras Carnival](/wiki/Patras_Carnival "Patras Carnival") is a three-day spectacle replete with concerts, theatre performances, parading troupes, an elaborate [treasure hunt](/wiki/Treasure_hunt_(game) "Treasure hunt (game)") game, three major parades, parallel celebrations specifically for children, and many [masquerade balls](/wiki/Masquerade_ball "Masquerade ball") including the famous Bourboúlia (Μπουρμπούλια) ball in which women wear special robe-like costumes called a *dómino* to hide their identy.[[183]](#cite_note-183) The festivities come to a crescendo on "Cheese Sunday" when The Grand Parade of troupes and floats leads celebrators to the harbor for the ceremonial burning of the effigy of King Carnival.[[184]](#cite_note-184)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Bourboulia_the_Ladies.JPG/250px-Bourboulia_the_Ladies.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bourboulia_the_Ladies.JPG)

Ladies on their way to the historic Bourboulia ball in Patras, Greece, wearing the *dómino* costumes unique to that ball

The Carnival in [Corfu](/wiki/Corfu_(city) "Corfu (city)") is much influenced by the Carnival of [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"). During this period, various theatrical sketches are presented on the island, called *Petególia* or *Petegolétsa* (Πετεγολέτσα) in the local dialect. In previous centuries, existed also the custom of "Giostra" ([jousting](/wiki/Jousting "Jousting")).[[185]](#cite_note-185)

The second biggest Carnival in Greece takes place in [Xanthi](/wiki/Xanthi "Xanthi") ([Eastern Macedonia and Thrace](/wiki/Eastern_Macedonia_and_Thrace "Eastern Macedonia and Thrace")) since 1966 and it is the major event of its kind in Northern Greece. The [Xanthi Carnival](/wiki/Xanthi_Carnival "Xanthi Carnival") manages to attract visitors from the nearby countries such as [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), and [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania").[[186]](#cite_note-186) Other regions host festivities of smaller extent, focused on the reenactment of traditional carnival customs, such as [Tyrnavos](/wiki/Tyrnavos "Tyrnavos") ([Thessaly](/wiki/Thessaly "Thessaly")), [Kozani](/wiki/Kozani "Kozani") ([Western Macedonia](/wiki/Western_Macedonia "Western Macedonia")), [Rethymno](/wiki/Rethymno "Rethymno") ([Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete")). Tyrnavos holds an annual Phallus festival, a traditional "phallkloric" event[[187]](#cite_note-187) in which giant, gaudily painted effigies of [phalluses](/wiki/Phallus "Phallus") made of [papier-mâché](/wiki/Papier-m%C3%A2ch%C3%A9 "Papier-mâché") are paraded, and which women are asked to touch or kiss. Their reward for so doing is a shot of the famous local [tsipouro](/wiki/Tsipouro "Tsipouro") alcohol spirit.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

Every year, from the 1st to the 8th of January, mostly in regions of [Western Macedonia](/wiki/Western_Macedonia "Western Macedonia"), traditional Carnival festivals erupt. Best known of these is the *Ragoutsária* (Ραγκουτσάρια)[[189]](#cite_note-189) festival in the city of [Kastoria](/wiki/Kastoria "Kastoria") whose celebration may date back to antiquity and whose name derives from the Latin word *rogatores* 'beggars', in reference to the beggars who could mingle with the rich in their masks.[[190]](#cite_note-:1-190) It takes place from 6–8 January with mass participation and is noted for its brass bands, flutes, and [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonians_(Greeks) "Macedonians (Greeks)") [drums](/wiki/Drums "Drums"). It is an ancient celebration of nature's rebirth akin to ancient festivals for [Dionysus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus") ([Dionysia](/wiki/Dionysia "Dionysia")) and [Kronos](/wiki/Cronus "Cronus") ([Saturnalia](/wiki/Saturnalia "Saturnalia")).[[190]](#cite_note-:1-190)

#### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Hungary")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Bus%C3%B3j%C3%A1r%C3%A1s_%28Moh%C3%A1cs%29%2C_2009.jpg/250px-Bus%C3%B3j%C3%A1r%C3%A1s_%28Moh%C3%A1cs%29%2C_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bus%C3%B3j%C3%A1r%C3%A1s_(Moh%C3%A1cs),_2009.jpg)

The [Busójárás](/wiki/Bus%C3%B3j%C3%A1r%C3%A1s "Busójárás") in [Mohács](/wiki/Moh%C3%A1cs "Mohács"), Hungary

Carnival season called "Farsang" in Hungarian happens between [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") (January 6) and [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") and is the time when costume parties traditionally take place, primarily for children (similar to [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween")).

In [Mohács](/wiki/Moh%C3%A1cs "Mohács"), Hungary, the *[Busójárás](/wiki/Bus%C3%B3j%C3%A1r%C3%A1s "Busójárás")* is a celebration held at the end of the Carnival season. It involves locals dressing in woolly costumes, with scary masks and noise-makers. According to legend, the festival celebrates both the conclusion of the winter season and a victory by the local people over invading Ottomans in 1526.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Italy")]

Main article: [Carnival in Italy](/wiki/Carnival_in_Italy "Carnival in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Venice_Carnival_-_Masked_Lovers_%282010%29.jpg/250px-Venice_Carnival_-_Masked_Lovers_%282010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Venice_Carnival_-_Masked_Lovers_(2010).jpg)

Distinctive [Venetian masks](/wiki/Venetian_mask "Venetian mask") at the [Carnival of Venice](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Carnevale_di_viareggio_2008%2C_uer_iz_de_party.JPG/250px-Carnevale_di_viareggio_2008%2C_uer_iz_de_party.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carnevale_di_viareggio_2008,_uer_iz_de_party.JPG)

[Carnival of Viareggio](/wiki/Carnival_of_Viareggio "Carnival of Viareggio") 2011, *Uer iz de parti?*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Dettaglio_della_battaglia_e_della_quantit%C3%A0_di_arance._%28foto_Baldo_Simone%29.jpg/250px-Dettaglio_della_battaglia_e_della_quantit%C3%A0_di_arance._%28foto_Baldo_Simone%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dettaglio_della_battaglia_e_della_quantit%C3%A0_di_arance._(foto_Baldo_Simone).jpg)

The [Battle of the Oranges](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Oranges "Battle of the Oranges") at the [Carnival of Ivrea](/wiki/Carnival_of_Ivrea "Carnival of Ivrea"), in [Ivrea](/wiki/Ivrea "Ivrea"), Italy

[Carnival in Italy](/wiki/Carnival_in_Italy "Carnival in Italy") is a farewell party to eat, drink, and have fun before the limitations and solemnity of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"). About a month before [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), Italians celebrate over many weekends with parades, masks, and *[confetti](/wiki/Confetti#Etymology_and_Italian_confetti "Confetti")*. The origins of this event may be traced to [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), when they worshipped [Bacchus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus") and [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn"). Some think they date back to archaic winter-to-spring ceremonies. Despite its pagan origins, the event was so extensively celebrated and the tradition so powerful that it was swiftly altered to fit into Catholic rituals. Carnival in Italy is traditionally celebrated on [Fat Tuesday](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras"), but the weekend prior features activities as well.

Carnival traditions vary across Italy. In the [Ambrosian rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_rite "Ambrosian rite") regions around Milan, Carnival ends on the first Sunday of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"). The [Carnival of Venice](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice") and [Carnival of Viareggio](/wiki/Carnival_of_Viareggio "Carnival of Viareggio") are particularly renowned, featuring sophisticated masquerades and parades. In [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), a distinct carnival form survives, possibly rooted in pre-Christian winter rituals of awakening the earth. These Carnivals include masquerades and parades.

The [Carnival in Venice](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice") was first documented in 1296, with a proclamation by the [Venetian Senate](/wiki/Venetian_Senate "Venetian Senate") announcing a public festival the day before the start of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"). Its subversive nature is reflected in Italy's many laws over the centuries attempting to restrict celebrations and the wearing of [masks](/wiki/Mask "Mask"). Carnival celebrations in Venice were halted after the city fell under [Austrian](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") control in 1798, but were revived in the late 20th century.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

The [Carnival of Viareggio](/wiki/Carnival_of_Viareggio "Carnival of Viareggio") is the second-most popular in Italy. It lasts a month with night and day celebrations, floats, parades, district celebrations, masked dances, and other shows. The first masquerade took place in 1873, in response to the upper classes' dissatisfaction with having to pay hefty taxes. Thousands of travelers go to Italy for parades, Carnival masks and costumes, concerts, and music.In 2001, the town built a new "Carnival citadel" dedicated to Carnival preparations and entertainment.[[193]](#cite_note-193)

The [Carnival of Ivrea](/wiki/Carnival_of_Ivrea "Carnival of Ivrea") is famous for its "[Battle of the Oranges](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Oranges "Battle of the Oranges")" fought with fruit between the people on foot and the troops of the tyrant on carts, to remember the wars of the Middle Ages,[[194]](#cite_note-194) allegory of struggle for freedom. It is considered one of the most ancient Carnivals in the world. The fight commemorates Ivrea's rebellion against tyrannical rule in the Middle Ages. The miller's daughter, "*la Mugnaia*", allegedly killed the city's dictator after he tried to kidnap her, sparking an uprising that gave the inhabitants more freedom. Ivrea's Carnival celebration now includes parades in medieval costumes, folkloric ensembles, and musical performances from Italy and Europe. A bean soup, *fagiolata*, is associated with the festival.

The [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite") ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *rito ambrosiano*)[[195]](#cite_note-195) is a [Latin liturgical rite](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") (specifically The Divine Liturgy of Saint Ambrose). The [rite](/wiki/Ritual_family "Ritual family") is named after [Saint Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose"), a [bishop of Milan](/wiki/Bishop_of_Milan "Bishop of Milan") in the fourth century. It is used by around five million Catholics in the greater part of the [Archdiocese of Milan](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Milan "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milan") (excluding [Monza](/wiki/Monza "Monza"), [Treviglio](/wiki/Treviglio "Treviglio") and [Trezzo sull'Adda](/wiki/Trezzo_sull%27Adda "Trezzo sull'Adda")), in some parishes of the [Diocese of Como](/wiki/Diocese_of_Como "Diocese of Como"), [Bergamo](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Bergamo "Roman Catholic Diocese of Bergamo"), [Novara](/wiki/Diocese_of_Novara "Diocese of Novara"), [Lodi](/wiki/Diocese_of_Lodi "Diocese of Lodi"), in the [Diocese of Lugano](/wiki/Diocese_of_Lugano "Diocese of Lugano"), [Canton of Ticino](/wiki/Canton_of_Ticino "Canton of Ticino"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), less prominently in some [Western Rite](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy") orthodox parishes and on special occasions of other jurisdictions.[[196]](#cite_note-196) In the most part of the [Archdiocese](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Milan "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milan") of [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), the Carnival lasts four more days, ending on the Saturday after Ash Wednesday, because of the Ambrosian Rite.[[197]](#cite_note-197)

In [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), the Carnival (in [Sardinian language](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") *Carrasecare* or *Carrasegare*[[198]](#cite_note-198)) varies greatly from the one in the mainland of Italy. the majority of the Sardinian celebrations features not only feasts and parades but also crude [fertility rites](/wiki/Fertility_rite "Fertility rite") such as bloodsheds to fertilize the land,[[199]](#cite_note-youtube.com-199) the death and the resurrection of the Carnival characters[[200]](#cite_note-200) and representations of violence and torture.[[199]](#cite_note-youtube.com-199) The typical characters of the Sardinian Carnival are [zoomorphic](/wiki/Zoomorphism "Zoomorphism") and/or [androgynous](/wiki/Androgyny "Androgyny"), such as the *Mamuthones and Issohadores*[[201]](#cite_note-201) from [Mamoiada](/wiki/Mamoiada "Mamoiada"), the *Boes and Merdules*[[202]](#cite_note-202) from [Ottana](/wiki/Ottana "Ottana") and many more.[[203]](#cite_note-203) The Carnival is celebrated with street performances[[204]](#cite_note-204) that are typically accompanied by Sardinian [dirges](/wiki/Dirge "Dirge") called *attittidus*,[[205]](#cite_note-205) meaning literally "cry of a baby when the mother doesn't want nursed him/her anymore" (from the word *titta* meaning breasts[[206]](#cite_note-206)). Other particular and important Carnival instances in Sardinia are the [Sartiglia](/wiki/Oristano#Sa_Sartiglia_and_other_events "Oristano") in [Oristano](/wiki/Oristano "Oristano") and the [Tempio Pausania](/wiki/Tempio_Pausania "Tempio Pausania") Carnival.[[207]](#cite_note-207)

#### Lithuania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Lithuania")]

Main article: [Užgavėnės](/wiki/U%C5%BEgav%C4%97n%C4%97s "Užgavėnės")

*Užgavėnės* is a [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") festival that takes place on Shrove Tuesday. Its name in English means "the time before Lent". The celebration corresponds to Carnival holiday traditions.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

*Užgavėnės* begins on the night before Ash Wednesday, when an [effigy](/wiki/Effigy "Effigy") of winter (usually named Morė) is burnt. A major element symbolizes the defeat of winter in the Northern Hemisphere. It is a staged battle between Lašininis ("porky") personifying winter and Kanapinis ("hempen man") personifying spring. Devils, witches, goats, the [grim reaper](/wiki/Grim_reaper "Grim reaper"), and other joyful and frightening characters appear in costumes during the celebrations.[[209]](#cite_note-:6-209)

Eating pancakes is an important part of the celebration. Eating pancakes during *Užgavėnės* is a traditional practice symbolizing the sun's return and the anticipation of a bountiful harvest, aligning with the festival's theme of bidding farewell to winter and welcoming spring.[[209]](#cite_note-:6-209)

#### Luxembourg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Luxembourg")]

In [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), the pre-Lenten holiday season is known as *Fuesend*. Throughout the Grand-Duchy, parades and parties are held.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

[Pétange](/wiki/P%C3%A9tange "Pétange") is the home of the Grand-Duchy's largest pre-Lenten Karneval celebration. Annually hosting a cavalcade with roughly 1,200 participants and thousand of celebrants, the official name is Karneval Gemeng Péiteng or "Kagepe" (the initials in [Luxembourgish](/wiki/Luxembourgish "Luxembourgish") are pronounced "Ka", "Ge" and "Pe").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The town of [Remich](/wiki/Remich "Remich") holds a three-day-long celebration, notable for two special events in addition to its parades. The first is the *Stréimännchen*, which is the burning of a male effigy from the Remich Bridge that crosses the [Moselle River](/wiki/Moselle_River "Moselle River") separating the Grand Duchy from [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). The *Stréimännchen* symbolizes the burning away of winter. The other special event at the Remich Fuesend celebrations is the *Buergbrennen* or [bonfire](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire") that closes the celebration.[[211]](#cite_note-211)

Like Remich, the town of [Esch-sur-Alzette](/wiki/Esch-sur-Alzette "Esch-sur-Alzette") holds a three-day celebration. Other major Fuesend parades in Luxembourg are held in the towns of [Diekirch](/wiki/Diekirch "Diekirch") and [Schifflange](/wiki/Schifflange "Schifflange").[[212]](#cite_note-212)

#### Malta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Malta")]

Main article: [Maltese Carnival](/wiki/Maltese_Carnival "Maltese Carnival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Carnival_in_Valletta_-_Trucks_in_Street_of_Valletta.jpg/250px-Carnival_in_Valletta_-_Trucks_in_Street_of_Valletta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnival_in_Valletta_-_Trucks_in_Street_of_Valletta.jpg)

Carnival procession in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta "Valletta"), Malta

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Carnival_in_Valletta.jpg/250px-Carnival_in_Valletta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnival_in_Valletta.jpg)

Carnival in Valletta, Malta

Carnival in [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") (Maltese: *il-Karnival ta' Malta*) was introduced to the islands by [Grand Master](/wiki/Grand_Master_(order) "Grand Master (order)") [Piero de Ponte](/wiki/Piero_de_Ponte "Piero de Ponte") in 1535. It is held during the week leading up to Ash Wednesday, and typically includes masked balls, fancy dresses, and grotesque mask competitions, lavish late-night parties, a colourful, ticker-tape parade of allegorical [floats](/wiki/Float_(parade) "Float (parade)") presided over by King Carnival (Maltese: *ir-Re tal-Karnival*), marching bands, and costumed revellers.[[213]](#cite_note-:3-213)

The largest celebration takes place in and around the capital city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta "Valletta") and [Floriana](/wiki/Floriana "Floriana"); several more "spontaneous" Carnivals take place in more remote areas. The [Nadur Carnival](/wiki/Nadur_Carnival "Nadur Carnival") is notable for its darker themes. In 2005, the Nadur Carnival hosted the largest-ever gathering of international Carnival organizers for the [FECC](/wiki/Federation_of_European_Carnival_Cities "Federation of European Carnival Cities")'s global summit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Traditional dances include the *parata*, a lighthearted re-enactment of the 1565 victory of the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller") over the [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), and an 18th-century court dance known as *il-Maltija*.[[213]](#cite_note-:3-213) Carnival food includes *perlini* (multi-coloured, sugar-coated almonds) and the *prinjolata*, which is a towering assembly of [sponge cake](/wiki/Sponge_cake "Sponge cake"), biscuits, almonds, and citrus fruits, topped with cream and pine nuts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Netherlands")]

Main article: [Carnival in the Netherlands](/wiki/Carnival_in_the_Netherlands "Carnival in the Netherlands")

[Carnival in the Netherlands](/wiki/Carnival_in_the_Netherlands "Carnival in the Netherlands") is called *Carnaval*, *Vastenavond* ("Eve of Lent") or, in [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish_language "Limburgish language"), *Vastelaovend(j)*, and is mostly celebrated in traditionally Catholic regions, particularly in the southern provinces of [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") and [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)"), but also in [Overijssel](/wiki/Overijssel "Overijssel"), especially in [Twente](/wiki/Twente "Twente"). While Dutch Carnaval is officially celebrated on the Sunday through Tuesday preceding Ash Wednesday, since the 1970s the feast has gradually started earlier and generally includes now the preceding weekend. Although traditions vary from town to town, Dutch carnaval usually includes a parade, a "Prince Carnival" plus [cortège](/wiki/Cort%C3%A8ge "Cortège") ("Council of 11", sometimes with a [Jester](/wiki/Jester "Jester") or Adjutant), sometimes also the handing over by the mayor of the symbolic keys of the town to Prince Carnival, the burning or burial of a symbolic figure, a [peasant wedding](/wiki/Wedding#Peasant_wedding "Wedding") (*boerenbruiloft*), and eating [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring") (*haring happen*) on Ash Wednesday.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Two main variants can be distinguished: the *[Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland") carnaval*, found in the province of Limburg, and the *[Bourgondische](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") carnaval*, found mainly in North Brabant. [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht"), Limburg's capital, holds a street carnaval that features elaborate costumes.[[214]](#cite_note-214)

The first known documentation dates from the late 8th century (*[Indiculus superstitionum et paganiarum](/wiki/Indiculus_superstitionum_et_paganiarum "Indiculus superstitionum et paganiarum")*), but Carnaval was already mentioned during the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in 325 and by [Caesarius of Arles](/wiki/Caesarius_of_Arles "Caesarius of Arles") (470–542) around 500 CE. In the Netherlands itself, the first documentation is found in 1383 in ['s-Hertogenbosch](/wiki/%27s-Hertogenbosch "'s-Hertogenbosch"). The oldest-known images of Dutch Carnaval festivities date from 1485, also in 's-Hertogenbosch.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Normal daily life comes to a stop for about a week in the southern part of the Netherlands during the carnival, with roads temporary blocked and many local businesses closed for the week as a result of employees who are en masse taking the days off during and the day after the carnival.[[215]](#cite_note-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216)

#### North Macedonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: North Macedonia")]

The most popular Carnivals in [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia") are in [Vevčani](/wiki/Vev%C4%8Dani "Vevčani") and [Strumica](/wiki/Strumica "Strumica").[[217]](#cite_note-217)

The Vevčani Carnival ([Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language"): Вевчански Kарневал, translated *Vevchanski Karneval*) has been held for over 1,400 years, and takes place on 13 and 14 January (New Year's Eve and New Year's Day by the old calendar). The village becomes a live theatre where costumed actors improvise on the streets in roles such as the traditional "August the Stupid".[[218]](#cite_note-218)

The Strumica Carnival (Macedonian: Струмички Карневал, translated *Strumichki Karneval*) has been held since at least 1670, when the Turkish author Evlija Chelebija wrote while staying there, "I came into a town located in the foothills of a high hillock and what I saw that night was masked people running house–to–house, with laughter, scream and song." The Carnival took an organized form in 1991; in 1994, Strumica became a member of [FECC](/wiki/Federation_of_European_Carnival_Cities "Federation of European Carnival Cities") and in 1998 hosted the XVIII International Congress of Carnival Cities. The Strumica Carnival opens on a Saturday night at a masked ball where the Prince and Princess are chosen; the main Carnival night is on Tuesday, when masked participants (including groups from abroad) compete in various subjects. As of 2000, the Festival of Caricatures and Aphorisms has been held as part of Strumica's Carnival celebrations.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

#### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Poland")]

See also: [Slavic carnival](/wiki/Slavic_carnival "Slavic carnival")

The [Polish](/wiki/Poland "Poland") Carnival season includes [Fat Thursday](/wiki/Fat_Thursday "Fat Thursday") (Polish: *Tłusty Czwartek*), when *[pączki](/wiki/P%C4%85czki "Pączki")* (doughnuts) are eaten, and *Śledzik* ([Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday")) or Herring Day. The Tuesday before the start of Lent is also often called *Ostatki* (literally "leftovers"), meaning the last day to party before the Lenten season.[[220]](#cite_note-220)

The traditional way to celebrate Carnival is the *[kulig](/wiki/Kulig "Kulig")*, a [horse-drawn sleigh](/wiki/Horse-drawn_sleigh "Horse-drawn sleigh") ride through the snow-covered countryside. In modern times, Carnival is increasingly seen as an excuse for intensive partying and has become more commercialized, with stores offering Carnival-season sales.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

#### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Portugal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Carnaval_de_Sesimbra_2007_24.JPG/250px-Carnaval_de_Sesimbra_2007_24.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carnaval_de_Sesimbra_2007_24.JPG)

Sesimbra Carnival, in [Sesimbra](/wiki/Sesimbra "Sesimbra"), Portugal

Carnival is Carnaval in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), celebrated throughout the country, most famously in [Ovar](/wiki/Ovar "Ovar"), [Sesimbra](/wiki/Sesimbra "Sesimbra"), [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), [Loulé](/wiki/Loul%C3%A9 "Loulé"), [Nazaré](/wiki/Nazar%C3%A9,_Portugal "Nazaré, Portugal"), [Mealhada](/wiki/Mealhada "Mealhada"), and [Torres Vedras](/wiki/Torres_Vedras "Torres Vedras"). Carnaval in [Podence](/wiki/Podence_(Macedo_de_Cavaleiros) "Podence (Macedo de Cavaleiros)") and [Lazarim](/wiki/Lazarim "Lazarim") incorporates pagan traditions such as the [careto](/wiki/Careto "Careto"), while the Torres Vedras celebration is probably the most typical.[[222]](#cite_note-222)

##### Azores

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Azores")]

On the islands of the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), local clubs and Carnival groups create colorful and creative costumes that jab at politics or culture.[[223]](#cite_note-:2-223)

On [São Miguel Island](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Miguel_Island "São Miguel Island"), Carnival features street vendors selling fried dough, called a [malassada](/wiki/Malassada "Malassada"). The festival on the biggest island starts off with a [black tie](/wiki/Black_tie "Black tie") grand ball, followed by Latin music at Coliseu Micaelense. A children's parade fills the streets of Ponta Delgada with children from each school district in costume. A massive parade continues past midnight, ending in [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks").[[223]](#cite_note-:2-223)

The event includes theatre performances and dances. In the "Danças de Entrudo", hundreds of people follow the dancers around the island. Throughout the show the dancers act out scenes from daily life. The "Dances de Carnival" are [allegorical](/wiki/Allegorical "Allegorical") and comedic tales acted out in the streets. The largest is in [Angra do Heroísmo](/wiki/Angra_do_Hero%C3%ADsmo "Angra do Heroísmo"), with more than 30 groups performing. More Portuguese-language theatrical performances occur there than anywhere else.[[223]](#cite_note-:2-223)

Festivities end on Ash Wednesday, when locals sit down for the "Batatada" or potato feast, in which the main dish is [salted cod](/wiki/Salted_cod "Salted cod") with potatoes, eggs, mint, bread and wine. Residents then return to the streets for the burning of the "Carnival clown", ending the season.[[223]](#cite_note-:2-223)

##### Madeira

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Madeira")]

Main article: [Carnival of Madeira](/wiki/Carnival_of_Madeira "Carnival of Madeira")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Funchal%2C_Cortejo_Aleg%C3%B3rico_de_Carnaval_%282026%29_07.jpg/250px-Funchal%2C_Cortejo_Aleg%C3%B3rico_de_Carnaval_%282026%29_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Funchal,_Cortejo_Aleg%C3%B3rico_de_Carnaval_(2026)_07.jpg)

A dancer in the [Carnival of Madeira](/wiki/Carnival_of_Madeira "Carnival of Madeira"), in [Funchal](/wiki/Funchal "Funchal"), Portugal

On the island of [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), the island's capital, [Funchal](/wiki/Funchal "Funchal"), wakes up on the Friday before Ash Wednesday to the sound of brass bands and Carnival parades throughout downtown. Festivities continue with concerts and shows in the Praça do Município for five consecutive days. The main Carnival street parade takes place on Saturday evening, with thousands of [samba](/wiki/Samba "Samba") dancers filling the streets. The traditional street event takes place on Tuesday, featuring daring caricatures.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

Arguably, Brazil's Carnival could be traced to the period of the [Portuguese Age of Discoveries](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery#Portuguese_exploration "Age of Discovery") when their [caravels](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel") passed regularly through Madeira, a territory that emphatically celebrated Carnival.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226)

##### Ovar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Ovar")]

Main article: [Carnival of Ovar](/wiki/Carnival_of_Ovar "Carnival of Ovar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Ovar_Portugal_Carnival.jpg/250px-Ovar_Portugal_Carnival.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ovar_Portugal_Carnival.jpg)

[Carnival of Ovar](/wiki/Carnival_of_Ovar "Carnival of Ovar") in [Ovar](/wiki/Ovar "Ovar"), Portugal

Carnival in the town of [Ovar](/wiki/Ovar "Ovar"), in the [Aveiro Region](/wiki/Regi%C3%A3o_de_Aveiro "Região de Aveiro"), began in 1952 and is the region's largest festivity, representing a large investment and the most important touristic event to the city. It is known for its creative designs, displayed in the Carnival Parade, which features troupes with themed costumes and music, ranging from the traditional to pop culture. Along with the Carnival Parade, there are five nights of partying, finishing with the famous 'Magical night' where people come from all over the country, mostly with their handmade costumes, only to have fun with the locals.[[227]](#cite_note-227)

##### Other regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: Other regions")]

In [Estarreja](/wiki/Estarreja "Estarreja"), in the Central region of Portugal, the town's first references to Carnival were in the 14th century, with "Flower Battles", richly decorated floats that paraded through the streets. At the beginning of the twentieth century, these festivities ended with the deaths of its main promoters, only to reappear again in the 1960s to become one of many important Carnival festivals in Portugal.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the Northern region of [Podence](/wiki/Podence_e_Santa_Combinha "Podence e Santa Combinha"), children appear from Sunday to Tuesday with tin masks and colorful multilayered costumes made from red, green and yellow wool. In the Central Portugal towns of [Nelas](/wiki/Nelas "Nelas") and [Canas de Senhorim](/wiki/Canas_de_Senhorim "Canas de Senhorim"), Carnival is an important tourist event. Nelas and Canas de Senhorim host four festive parades that offer colorful and creative costumes: Bairro da Igreja and Cimo do Povo in Nelas and do Paço and do Rossio in Canas de Senhorim.[[228]](#cite_note-228)

In [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), Carnival offers parades, dances and festivities featuring stars from Portugal and Brazil. The [Loures](/wiki/Loures "Loures") Carnival celebrates the country's folk traditions, including the *enterro do bacalhau* or burial of the [cod](/wiki/Cod "Cod"), which marks the end of Carnival and the festivities.[[229]](#cite_note-229)

North of Lisbon is the famous [Torres Vedras](/wiki/Torres_Vedras "Torres Vedras") Carnival, described as the "most Portuguese in Portugal". The celebration highlight is a parade of creatively decorated streetcars that satirize society and politics.[[230]](#cite_note-230)

Other Central Portugal towns, such as [Fátima](/wiki/F%C3%A1tima,_Portugal "Fátima, Portugal") and [Leiria](/wiki/Leiria "Leiria"), offer colorful, family-friendly celebrations. In these towns, everyone dresses up as if it were [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween"). Children and adults wear masks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve") region, several resort towns offer Carnival parades. Besides the themed floats and cars, the festivities include "samba" groups, bands, dances, and music.[[231]](#cite_note-231)

In [Lazarim](/wiki/Lazarim "Lazarim"), a civil parish in the municipality of [Lamego](/wiki/Lamego "Lamego"), celebrations follow the pagan tradition of Roman [Saturnalias](/wiki/Saturnalia "Saturnalia"). It celebrates by burning colorful effigies and dressing in home-made costumes. Locally-made wooden masks are worn. The masks are effigies of men and women with horns, but both roles are performed by men. They are distinguished by their clothes, with caricature attributes of both men and women.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Lazarim Carnaval cycle encompasses two periods, the first starting on the fifth Sunday before Quinquagesima Sunday. Masked figures and people wearing large sculpted heads walk through the town. The locals feast on meats, above all [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork"). The second cycle, held on Sundays preceding Ash Wednesday, incorporates the tradition of the Compadres and Comadres, with men and women displaying light-hearted authority over the other.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Over the five weeks, men prepare large masked heads and women raise funds to pay for two [mannequins](/wiki/Mannequin "Mannequin") that will be sacrificed in a public bonfire.
