This is a key event and is unique to Portugal. During the bonfire, a girl reads the Compadre's will and a boy reads the Comadre's will. The executors of the will are named, a [donkey](/wiki/Donkey "Donkey") is symbolically distributed to both female and male "heirs", and then there is the final reckoning in which the Entrudo, or Carnival doll, is burned.[[232]](#cite_note-232)

#### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main article: [Maslenitsa](/wiki/Maslenitsa "Maslenitsa")

See also: [Slavic carnival](/wiki/Slavic_carnival "Slavic carnival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Shrovetide._Kustodiev.jpg/250px-Shrovetide._Kustodiev.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shrovetide._Kustodiev.jpg)

[Boris Kustodiev](/wiki/Boris_Kustodiev "Boris Kustodiev")'s painting of [Maslenitsa](/wiki/Maslenitsa "Maslenitsa"), Russia

[Maslenitsa](/wiki/Maslenitsa "Maslenitsa") (Масленица, also called "Pancake Week" or "Cheese Week") is a Russian folk holiday that incorporates some pagan traditions. It is celebrated during the last week before Lent. The essential element is [bliny](/wiki/Bliny "Bliny"), Russian pancakes, popularly taken to symbolize the sun. Round and golden, they are made from the rich foods allowed that week by the Orthodox traditions: butter, eggs, and milk. (In the tradition of Orthodox Lent, the consumption of meat ceases one week before that of milk and eggs.)[[233]](#cite_note-233)

Maslenitsa also includes [masquerades](/wiki/Masquerade_ball "Masquerade ball"), snowball fights, sledding, swinging on swings, and sleigh rides. The mascot is a brightly dressed straw effigy of Lady Maslenitsa, formerly known as [Kostroma](/wiki/Kostroma_(deity) "Kostroma (deity)"). The celebration culminates on Sunday evening, when Lady Maslenitsa is stripped of her finery and put to the flames of a [bonfire](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire").[[234]](#cite_note-234)

#### Slovakia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Slovakia")]

See also: [Slavic carnival](/wiki/Slavic_carnival "Slavic carnival")

In [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), the [Fašiangy](/wiki/Fa%C5%A1iangy "Fašiangy") (*fašiang*, *fašangy*) takes place from [Three Kings Day](/wiki/Three_Kings_Day "Three Kings Day") (*Traja králi*) until the midnight before Ash Wednesday (*Škaredá streda* or *Popolcová streda*). At the midnight marking the end of *fašiangy*, a symbolic burial ceremony for the [contrabass](/wiki/Contrabass "Contrabass") is performed, because music ceases for [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").[[235]](#cite_note-235)

#### Slovenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Slovenia")]

The Slovenian name for carnival is *pust*. The [Slovenian](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") countryside displays a variety of disguised groups and individual characters, among which the most popular and characteristic is the Kurent (plural: *Kurenti*), a monstrous and demon-like, but fluffy figure. The most significant festival is held in [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj") (see: [Kurentovanje](/wiki/Kurentovanje "Kurentovanje")). Its special feature are the Kurents themselves, magical creatures from another world, who visit major events throughout the country, trying to banish the winter and announce spring's arrival, fertility, and new life with noise and dancing. The origin of the Kurent is a mystery, and not much is known of the times, beliefs, or purposes connected with its first appearance. The origin of the name itself is obscure.[[236]](#cite_note-236)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Ale%C5%A1_Kravos_Kurentovanje_Ptuj_2019.jpg/250px-Ale%C5%A1_Kravos_Kurentovanje_Ptuj_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ale%C5%A1_Kravos_Kurentovanje_Ptuj_2019.jpg)

[Kurentovanje](/wiki/Kurentovanje "Kurentovanje") in [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj"), Slovenia

The [Cerknica](/wiki/Cerknica "Cerknica") Carnival is heralded by a figure called "Poganjič" carrying a whip. In the procession, organised by the "Pust society", a monstrous witch named Uršula is driven from the mountain [Slivnica](/wiki/Slivnica_(mountain) "Slivnica (mountain)"), to be burned at the stake on Ash Wednesday. Unique to this region is a group of [dormice](/wiki/Dormice "Dormice"), driven by the Devil and a huge fire-breathing dragon. [Cerkno](/wiki/Cerkno "Cerkno") and its surrounding area are known for the *Laufarji*, Carnival figures with artistically carved wooden masks.[[237]](#cite_note-237)

The *Maškare* from [Dobrepolje](/wiki/Dobrepolje "Dobrepolje") used to represent a triple character: the beautiful, the ugly (among which the most important represented by an old man, an old woman, a hunchback, and a *Kurent*), and the noble (imitating the urban elite).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The major part of the population, especially the young and children, dress up in ordinary non-ethnic costumes, going to school, work, and organized events, where prizes are given for the best and most original costumes. Costumed children sometimes go from house to house asking for treats.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=83 "Edit section: Spain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/GOYA_-_Entierro_de_la_Sardina_%28Real_Academia_de_Bellas_Artes_de_San_Fernando%2C_1812-14%29.jpg/250px-GOYA_-_Entierro_de_la_Sardina_%28Real_Academia_de_Bellas_Artes_de_San_Fernando%2C_1812-14%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:GOYA_-_Entierro_de_la_Sardina_(Real_Academia_de_Bellas_Artes_de_San_Fernando,_1812-14).jpg)

*[The Burial of the Sardine](/wiki/The_Burial_of_the_Sardine "The Burial of the Sardine")*, Francisco Goya, c. 1812

Arguably the most famous Carnivals in Spain are [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), [Las Palmas](/wiki/Las_Palmas "Las Palmas"), [Sitges](/wiki/Sitges "Sitges"), [Vilanova i la Geltrú](/wiki/Vilanova_i_la_Geltr%C3%BA "Vilanova i la Geltrú"), [Tarragona](/wiki/Tarragona "Tarragona"), [Águilas](/wiki/%C3%81guilas "Águilas"), [Solsona](/wiki/Solsona,_Lleida "Solsona, Lleida"), [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz"), [Badajoz](/wiki/Badajoz "Badajoz"), [Bielsa](/wiki/Bielsa "Bielsa") (an ancestral Carnival celebration), [Plan](/wiki/Plan,_Aragon "Plan, Aragon"), [San Juan de Plan](/wiki/San_Juan_de_Plan "San Juan de Plan"), [Laza](/wiki/Laza,_Spain "Laza, Spain"), [Verín](/wiki/Ver%C3%ADn "Verín"), Viana, and [Xinzo de Limia](/wiki/Xinzo_de_Limia "Xinzo de Limia").[[238]](#cite_note-238)

One of the oldest pre-Indo-European carnival in Europe takes place in [Ituren](/wiki/Ituren "Ituren") and [Zubieta](/wiki/Zubieta "Zubieta") in [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre") in late January/early February. The carnival symbolises the eternal struggle between the forces of good and evil, light and darkness, winter and spring.

##### Andalusia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=84 "Edit section: Andalusia")]

Main article: [Carnival of Cádiz](/wiki/Carnival_of_C%C3%A1diz "Carnival of Cádiz")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/86/Carnavaldecadiz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnavaldecadiz.jpg)

A choir singing in the [Carnival of Cádiz](/wiki/Carnival_of_C%C3%A1diz "Carnival of Cádiz"), in [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz"), [Andalusia](/wiki/Andalusia "Andalusia"), Spain

In [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz"), the costumes worn are often related to recent news, such as the [bird flu](/wiki/Influenza_A_virus_subtype_H5N1 "Influenza A virus subtype H5N1") epidemic in 2006, during which many people were disguised as chickens. The feeling of this Carnival is the sharp criticism, the funny play on words and the imagination in the costumes, more than the glamorous dressings. It is traditional to paint the face with [lipstick](/wiki/Lipstick "Lipstick") as a humble substitute of a mask.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

The most famous groups are the [chirigotas](/wiki/Chirigota "Chirigota"), choirs, and [comparsas](/wiki/Comparsa "Comparsa"). The chirigotas are well known witty, satiric popular groups who sing about politics, new times, and household topics, wearing the same costume, which they prepare for the whole year. The Choirs (*coros*) are wider groups that go on open carts through the streets singing with an orchestra of guitars and lutes. Their signature piece is the "Carnival Tango", alternating comical and serious repertory. The comparsas are the serious counterpart of the chirigota in Cádiz, and the poetic lyrics and the criticism are their main ingredients. They have a more elaborated [polyphony](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") that is easily recognizable by the typical [countertenor](/wiki/Countertenor "Countertenor") voice.[[240]](#cite_note-240)

##### Canary Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=85 "Edit section: Canary Islands")]

See also: [Carnival of Las Palmas](/wiki/Carnival_of_Las_Palmas "Carnival of Las Palmas") and [Carnival of Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Carnival_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Carnival of Santa Cruz de Tenerife")

The Santa Cruz Carnival is the most important festival for Spanish tourism and Spain's largest Carnival.[[241]](#cite_note-241)[[242]](#cite_note-242)[[243]](#cite_note-243)[[244]](#cite_note-244) In 1980, it was declared a Festival Tourist International Interest. Every February, [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), the capital of the largest of the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), hosts the event, attracting around a million people.[[245]](#cite_note-santacruzmas.com-245)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Contestant_Angeles_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Paez_performs_during_the_Tenerife_Carnival_Queen_2009_contest_in_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife.jpg/250px-Contestant_Angeles_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Paez_performs_during_the_Tenerife_Carnival_Queen_2009_contest_in_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife.jpg)](/wiki/File:Contestant_Angeles_Hern%C3%A1ndez_Paez_performs_during_the_Tenerife_Carnival_Queen_2009_contest_in_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife.jpg)

Carnival Queen of [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), Spain

In 1980, it was declared a Festival Tourist International Interest.[[245]](#cite_note-santacruzmas.com-245) In 1987, [Cuban](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") singer [Celia Cruz](/wiki/Celia_Cruz "Celia Cruz") with orchestra [Billo's Caracas Boys](/wiki/Billo%27s_Caracas_Boys "Billo's Caracas Boys") performed at the "Carnival Chicharrero", attended by 250,000 people. This was registered in the *[Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records")* as the largest gathering of people in an outdoor plaza to attend a concert, a record she holds today.[[246]](#cite_note-246)

The Carnival of [Las Palmas](/wiki/Las_Palmas "Las Palmas") ([Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria")) has a drag queen's gala where a jury chooses a winner.[[247]](#cite_note-247)

##### Catalonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=86 "Edit section: Catalonia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Vidalot.jpg/250px-Vidalot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vidalot.jpg)

*Vidalot* is the last night of revelry before Ash Wednesday in [Vilanova](/wiki/Vilanova_i_la_Geltr%C3%BA "Vilanova i la Geltrú"), [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), Spain. Water color painting by Brad Erickson.

In [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), people dress in masks and costume (often in themed groups) and organize a week-long series of parties, pranks, outlandish activities such as bed races, street dramas satirizing public figures, and raucous processions to welcome the arrival of *Sa Majestat el Rei Carnestoltes* ("His Majesty King Carnival"), known by various titles, including *el Rei dels poca-soltes* ("King of the crackpots"), *Princep etern de Cornudella* ("eternal Prince of Cuckoldry"), *Duc de ximples i corrumputs* ("Duke of the fool and corrupt"), *Marquès de la bona mamella* ("Marquis of the lovely breast"), *Comte de tots els barruts* ("Count of the insolent"), *Baró de les Calaverades* ("Baron of mocturnal debaucheries"), and *Senyor de l'alt Plàtan florit, dels barraquers i gamberrades i artista d'honor dalt del llit* ("Lord of the tall blooming Banana, of the voyeurs and punks and the artist of honor upon the bed").[[248]](#cite_note-248)

The King presides over a period of misrule in which conventional social rules may be broken and reckless behavior is encouraged. Festivities are held in the open air, beginning with a *cercavila*, a ritual procession throughout the town to call everyone to attend. *Rues* of masked revelers dance alongside. On Thursday, *Dijous Gras* (Fat Thursday) is celebrated, also called 'omelette day' (el *dia de la truita*), on which *[coques](/wiki/Coca_(pastry) "Coca (pastry)")* (*de llardons, butifarra d'ou, butifarra*), and [omelettes](/wiki/Omelette "Omelette") are eaten. The festivities end on Ash Wednesday with elaborate funeral rituals marking the death of King Carnival, who is typically burned on a [pyre](/wiki/Pyre "Pyre") in what is called the "burial of the [sardine](/wiki/Sardine "Sardine")" (*enterrament de la sardina*), or, in Vilanova, as *l'enterro*.[[249]](#cite_note-Erickson,_Brad_2008-249)

The Carnival of [Vilanova i la Geltrú](/wiki/Vilanova_i_la_Geltr%C3%BA "Vilanova i la Geltrú") has a documented history from 1790[[250]](#cite_note-Garcia,_Xavier_1972-250) and is one of the richest in the variety of its acts and rituals. It adopts an ancient style in which satire, the [grotesque body](/wiki/Grotesque_body "Grotesque body")[[4]](#cite_note-Bakhtin-4) (particularly [cross-dressing](/wiki/Cross-dressing "Cross-dressing") and displays of exaggerated bellies, noses, and phalli), and, above all, active participation are valued over glamorous, media-friendly spectacles that Vilanovins mock as "thighs and feathers".[[251]](#cite_note-251) It is best known for *Les Comparses*, a tumultuous dance held on Sunday in which thousands of dancers in traditional dresses and *Mantons de Manila* (Manila Shawls), organized into groups of couples march in the street forming lines while throwing tons of hard candies at one another. Vilanovinians organize several rituals during the week. On *Dijous Gras,* Vilanovin children are excused from school to participate in the *Merengada,* a day-long scene of eating and fighting with sticky, sweet [meringue](/wiki/Meringue "Meringue") while adults have a meringue battle at midnight at the historic *Plaça de les Cols*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Merengada.jpg/250px-Merengada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Merengada.jpg)

Children become covered in meringue during Dijous Gras, in Vilanova, Catalonia, Spain

On Friday citizens are called to a parade for the arrival of King Carnival called *l'Arrivo* that changes every year. It includes a raucous procession of floats and dancers lampooning current events or public figures and a bitingly satiric sermon (*el sermo*) delivered by the King himself. On Saturday, the King's procession and his [concubines](/wiki/Concubine "Concubine") scandalize the town with their sexual behavior; the mysterious *Moixo Foguer* (Little-Bird-Bonfire) is shown accompanied by the *Xerraire* (jabberer), who try to convince the crowd about the wonders that this mighty bird carries in a box (which ends up being in fact a naked person covered in feathers);[[252]](#cite_note-252) and other items such as: sport acts and barbecues in the streets; the *talking-dance* of the Mismatched Couples (*Ball de Malcasats*); the children's King *Caramel* whose massive belly, long nose and sausage-like hair hint at his insatiable appetites; or the debauched *Nit dels Mascarots* dance.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Malcasats.jpg/250px-Malcasats.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malcasats.jpg)

The Ball de Malcasats (Dance of the Mismatched Couples) is a satiric talking-dance traditional to Carnaval in Vilanova, Catalonia, Spain.

After Sunday, vilanovinians continue its Carnival with the children's party *Vidalet*, the satirical chorus of Carnestoltes songs and the last night of revelry, the *Vidalot.* For the King's funeral, people dress in elaborate mourning costume, many of them cross-dressing men who carry bouquets of phallic vegetables. In the funeral house, the body of the King is surrounded by weeping concubines, crying over the loss of sexual pleasure brought about by his death. The King's body is carried to the *Plaça de la Vila* where a satiric eulogy is delivered while the townspeople eat salty grilled sardines with bread and wine, suggesting the symbolic [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") of the communion ritual. Finally, amid rockets and explosions, the King's body is burned in a massive pyre.[[249]](#cite_note-Erickson,_Brad_2008-249)[[250]](#cite_note-Garcia,_Xavier_1972-250)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Rucs_de_Solsona.jpg/250px-Rucs_de_Solsona.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rucs_de_Solsona.jpg)

Donkeys of [Solsona](/wiki/Solsona,_Lleida "Solsona, Lleida"), Catalonia, Spain, hung in the tower bell

Carnaval de Solsona takes place in [Solsona, Lleida](/wiki/Solsona,_Lleida "Solsona, Lleida"). It is one of the longest; free events in the streets and nightly concerts run for more than a week. The Carnival is known for a legend that explains how a donkey was hung at the tower bell − because the animal wanted to eat grass that grew on the top of the tower. To celebrate this legend, locals hang a stuffed donkey at the tower that "pisses" above the excited crowd using a water pump. This event is the most important and takes place on Saturday night. For this reason, the inhabitants are called *matarrucs* ("donkey killers").[[253]](#cite_note-253)

"Comparses" groups organize free activities. These groups of friends create and personalize a uniformed suit to wear during the festivities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In [Sitges](/wiki/Sitges "Sitges"), special feasts include *xatonades* (*[xató](/wiki/Xat%C3%B3 "Xató")* is a traditional local salad of the Penedès coast) served with omelettes. Two important moments are the *Rua de la Disbauxa* (Debauchery Parade) on Sunday night and the *Rua de l'Extermini* (Extermination Parade) on Tuesday night.[[254]](#cite_note-254) In 2022, the Rua de la Disbauxa hosted 34 floats and over 1,200 participants.[[255]](#cite_note-255)

[Tarragona](/wiki/Tarragona "Tarragona") has one of the region's most complete ritual sequences. The events start with the building of a huge barrel and ends with its burning with the effigies of the King and Queen. On Saturday, the main parade takes place with masked groups, zoomorphic figures, music, and percussion bands, and groups with [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks") (the devils, the dragon, the ox, the female dragon). Carnival groups stand out for their clothes full of elegance, showing brilliant examples of fabric crafts, at the Saturday and Sunday parades. About 5,000 people are members of the parade groups.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### Galicia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=87 "Edit section: Galicia")]

In [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"), the celebration is commonly known as *Entroido*, or otherwise *Antroido*, *Entroiro*, *Entruido* or *Entrudio*.[[256]](#cite_note-256) Throughout history, the cities, towns and villages of Galicia had poor connections between each other, so *Entroidos* are very diverse with different cultural traditions and characters based in the zone you're in. Nine Entroido celebrations have been declared as places of touristic interest in Galicia: [Xinzo de Limia](/wiki/Xinzo_de_Limia "Xinzo de Limia"), [Cobres](/w/index.php?title=Santo_Adr%C3%A1n_de_Cobres&action=edit&redlink=1 "Santo Adrán de Cobres (page does not exist)"), [Laza](/wiki/Laza,_Spain "Laza, Spain"), [Verín](/wiki/Ver%C3%ADn "Verín"), [Felos de Maceda](/w/index.php?title=Felos_de_Maceda&action=edit&redlink=1 "Felos de Maceda (page does not exist)"), [Xenerais do Ulla](/w/index.php?title=Xenerais_do_Ulla&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xenerais do Ulla (page does not exist)"), [Vilariño de Conso](/wiki/Vilari%C3%B1o_de_Conso "Vilariño de Conso"), [Androlla](/wiki/Androlla "Androlla") and [Viana do Bolo](/wiki/Viana_do_Bolo "Viana do Bolo") and [Manzaneda](/wiki/Manzaneda "Manzaneda").[[257]](#cite_note-257)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Cigarrones.jpg/250px-Cigarrones.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cigarrones.jpg)

The *Cigarrón*, the typical character of the Entroido, in [Verín](/wiki/Ver%C3%ADn "Verín"), [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"), Spain

In [Galician cuisine](/wiki/Galician_cuisine "Galician cuisine"), there are various traditional dishes of *Entroido*, such as *Filloas* (a dish similar to [crêpes](/wiki/Cr%C3%AApe "Crêpe")) and *Orellas* (A traditional dish of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") and the [Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community"), but also traditionally served in Galician and [French](/wiki/France "France") carnivals). Other traditional foods include [Chorizo](/wiki/Chorizo "Chorizo"), [Lacón](/wiki/Lac%C3%B3n "Lacón"), [Caldo](/wiki/Broth "Broth"), [Androlla](/wiki/Androlla "Androlla") and [Pig Heads](/wiki/Pork "Pork").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Filloas.jpg/250px-Filloas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Filloas.jpg)

*Filloas*, a [crêpe](/wiki/Cr%C3%AApe "Crêpe")-like typical dish served in Galician Entroidos

##### Valencian Community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=88 "Edit section: Valencian Community")]

One of the most important Spanish Carnival Festivals is celebrated in [Vinaròs](/wiki/Vinar%C3%B2s "Vinaròs"), a small town situated in the northern part of the [province of Castellón](/wiki/Province_of_Castell%C3%B3n "Province of Castellón"), [Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community").

The Carnival Festival in Vinaròs has been declared of Regional Touristic Interest and in 2017, this outstanding and ancient show celebrated 35 years of History. The Carnival Festival in Vinaròs became a forbidden celebration during the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") but after the dictatorship, the party regained importance with the democracy's arrival.

Every year in February, forty days before the Spanish [Cuaresma](/wiki/Cuaresma "Cuaresma"), thirty-three "comparsas" go singing, dancing and walking down the streets in a great costumes' parade in Vinaròs. In addition, many other festive, cultural and musical activities of all ages take place, such as an epic battle of confetti and flour, funny karaoke contests or the so-called "[Entierro de la Sardina](/wiki/Entierro_de_la_Sardina "Entierro de la Sardina")" (Burial of the Sardine).

Nevertheless, the most important event is the gala performance of the Carnival's Queen. In this breathtaking show, it is elected the Queen of the Carnival, the major representative of the Carnival in Vinaròs all year round.[[258]](#cite_note-258)

#### Turkey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=89 "Edit section: Turkey")]

Main article: [Baklahorani](/wiki/Baklahorani "Baklahorani")

For almost five centuries, [local Greek](/wiki/Greeks_in_Turkey "Greeks in Turkey") [Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") communities throughout [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul") celebrated Carnival with weeks of bawdy parades, lavish balls, and street parties.[[259]](#cite_note-GreekMinority-259) This continued for weeks before Lent. [Baklahorani](/wiki/Baklahorani "Baklahorani") took place on [Shrove Monday](/wiki/Shrove_Monday "Shrove Monday") or [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"), the last day of the carnival season. The event was led by the Greek Orthodox community, but the celebrations were public and inter-communal. The final celebration was sited in the [Kurtuluş](/wiki/Kurtulu%C5%9F "Kurtuluş") district.[[260]](#cite_note-260) In 2010, the festival was revived.[[261]](#cite_note-261)

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carnival&action=edit&section=90 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday")

In [England](/wiki/England "England"), the three days before Lent were called Shrovetide. A time for [confessing sins](/wiki/Confession_(religion) "Confession (religion)") ("shriving"), it had fewer festivities than the continental Carnivals. Today, Shrove Tuesday is celebrated as Pancake Day, but little else of the Lent-related Shrovetide survived the 16th-century [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation").

Since 2012, Hastings in East Sussex has celebrated with its own Mardi Gras, or Fat Tuesday. Five days of music and street events culminating with a Grand Ball on Fat Tuesday itself. Loosely based on the New Orleans style of carnival, Hastings has taken its own course.[[262]](#cite_note-262)
